prose.body: Most peptides get destroyed by stomach acid within minutes. This one doesn't. That's the entire reason it exists as a research target in the first place. The polyproline motif at positions 3-5 makes it resistant to proteolytic cleavage. Unusual stability = oral bioavailability = possible therapeutic route that doesn't require injection.

Sikirić is named on BPC-157 patent applications dating back to 1989. Croatian government records list him as an owner of PharmaCotherapia (a company that sponsored a never-completed human trial in Tijuana) and as CEO of Diagen (which claims a patent on a "special stable version" of BPC-157). This is not unusual for academic researchers who commercialize their work. Critics argue these interests go undisclosed in papers and may introduce bias.

Pharmaceutical regulators and most scientists don't trust data from a single source. Not because it's automatically wrong (Sikirić's work is peer-reviewed and methodologically standard) but because independent replication is how science becomes durable. A Polish research group published critical responses in the late 2010s. Sikirić published detailed rebuttals. The back-and-forth is ongoing. One recent review author told STAT in 2026 that BPC-157 "should not be used by humans" until independent trials exist. That's the scientific establishment's position.

The human trial record is scattered rather than cleanly failed: one abandoned or partial IBD program, one older phase 1 registry record with no posted results, one tiny IV safety pilot, and one ongoing hamstring-strain trial.
Aggregated from r/Peptides, r/Bodybuilding, r/PeptideScience, peptide clinics, athlete interviews, 2019-2026. Uncontrolled. Selection bias severe:

B is for inconsistent compounds. BPC is more consistent than that. Other A-tier compounds with similar evidence quality (TB-500, GHK-Cu) show similar outcomes in reported use. The A tier is for "works for most people, limited by lack of human trials rather than by unpredictability."

The side-effect story is less characterized than the internet makes it sound. No obvious hormonal-suppression signal, no addiction signal, and small pilots have not raised a red flag, but long-term human safety is not mapped. Product identity and sterility in gray-market vials are part of the risk, not a side issue.

The preclinical base keeps widening. BPC-157 now has documented effects well past tendon and GI healing: remote-organ protection in ischemia/reperfusion injury, vascular protection models, and a growing cytoprotection thesis. A 2025 review organizes the literature around therapy and safety as one question, which is the right frame. The gap that remains is regulatory-grade human PK, efficacy, and safety work. That gap is not cosmetic. It is the whole reason the page stops short of S-tier.

the newer BPC literature keeps pushing the musculoskeletal case more than the GI one. a 2026 review on tendon, ligament, muscle, and junctional injury argues that the repair signal across models is still unusually consistent, but the clinical layer is still thin. so the basic tension here hasn't changed, more depth, still not enough human proof.

The core credit for semaglutide goes to Lotte Bjerre Knudsen, a Novo Nordisk chemist who led the GLP-1 program starting in the 1990s. Her earlier work produced liraglutide (Victoza for diabetes, 2010; Saxenda for obesity, 2014), the proof-of-concept that a modified GLP-1 could work as a daily injection. Semaglutide was the next generation: longer half-life, stronger effect, weekly injection instead of daily. Knudsen is now widely regarded as the scientific mother of the GLP-1 class.

By April 2026 that market cap is down to roughly $180 billion, a 70% collapse in under two years. Three things hit simultaneously: (1) Eli Lilly's tirzepatide beat semaglutide head-to-head in SURMOUNT-5, (2) tirzepatide became the world's best-selling drug in Q3 2025 at $10.1 billion a quarter, and (3) the Trump administration negotiated significant price cuts starting 2026 via the CMS "BALANCE" pilot program. Novo laid off 9,000 workers, 9% of its workforce, in September 2025. CEO Lars Fruergaard stepped down and was replaced by Maziar Mike Doustdar.

Eli Lilly hit a $1 trillion market cap in 2025, the first pharmaceutical company ever to do so, on the back of tirzepatide alone. Lilly reported $36.5 billion in combined Mounjaro + Zepbound revenue in 2025 vs Novo's $33 billion for semaglutide. Zepbound now holds 71% of new U.S. obesity prescriptions. Novo's response has been price aggression (NovoCare direct-to-consumer at $499/month from a list price near $1,300), label expansion (cardiovascular, HFpEF, osteoarthritis), and an April 2026 partnership with OpenAI for AI-driven drug discovery. Whether any of it works is the 2026 question.

Public companies in adjacent industries started disclosing GLP-1 impact in earnings calls starting 2023. Major restaurant chains saw flat or declining same-store traffic among frequent visitors. Snack food manufacturers revised guidance downward. J.P. Morgan estimated in 2026 that GLP-1s could remove $30-55 billion per year from the food & beverage industry by 2030, with GLP-1 users consuming 21% fewer calories and spending 31% less on groceries.

Gastroparesis (paralyzed stomach) lawsuits began in August 2023 with a Louisiana plaintiff, Jaclyn Bjorklund. By February 2024, 55 federal cases were consolidated into multidistrict litigation targeting Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly. Plaintiffs allege the labels inadequately warned about severe gastroparesis requiring hospitalization. Cases are ongoing. Separately, ongoing class actions allege unfair pricing and conspiracy with pharmacy benefit managers. None of this is unusual for a drug at this scale of exposure, but it's the first wave of a long legal tail.

GLP-1 receptor agonism does four things simultaneously: slows gastric emptying (food sits in your stomach longer), increases insulin release when blood glucose is high, suppresses glucagon, and acts on hypothalamic satiety centers to reduce appetite. The combined effect is that you feel full faster, stay full longer, and don't think about food the way you used to.

GI side effects are the story. Nausea, diarrhea, constipation, vomiting, 74-87% of users depending on the trial. Most are dose-dependent and wane after 4-8 weeks as tolerance develops. The standard titration (0.25 → 0.5 → 1.0 → 1.7 → 2.4 mg) exists specifically to manage this.

Two regulatory updates from the post-marketing surveillance program belong in the safety story. In 2024 the FDA opened a formal evaluation of suicidal-thoughts-and-behaviors reports across the GLP-1 receptor agonist class after early signals from EMA databases. The agency completed that review in 2026 and concluded that a labeled warning was not supported by the cumulative evidence; FDA requested removal of the suicidal-behavior-and-ideation language from GLP-1 class labels. Separately, EMA continues to monitor the NAION optic-nerve signal that surfaced through European pharmacovigilance. the signal is still considered preliminary, with no causal link established and no labeled warning yet on either side of the atlantic. neither update changes the editorial verdict; both belong on the page because they are part of the safety record this drug is now accountable to.

Semaglutide works while you're on it. Once you stop, most of the weight comes back. STEP-4 showed participants regained two-thirds of their lost weight within 12 months of discontinuation. This is not a failure of the drug. It's how appetite regulation works. Sema suppresses the appetite signal; remove it, appetite returns. The practical implication: this is a long-term drug, possibly lifelong, not a cure.

prose.header: gray market, is it the same molecule
prose.body: Sometimes. Research-grade semaglutide sold online is, in some cases, chemically identical to Ozempic/Wegovy. In other cases it's not. Without a third-party lab certificate of analysis (COA), you can't tell. University-run testing on gray-market samples has found: correct molecule but inconsistent concentration (overdose risk), different peptide entirely, bacterial contamination. ~2 million Americans used compounded sema between late 2023 and late 2024 before FDA enforcement tightened.

Semaglutide stopped being only a weight-loss drug faster than most expected. The FLOW program established kidney-outcome benefit in chronic kidney disease with type 2 diabetes. It's now part of the CKD conversation, not just obesity medicine. Real-world data separating oral from injectable semaglutide suggests the formulation difference is more than route, adherence, effectiveness, and tolerability differ in ways that matter clinically. And an observational signal around alcohol use disorder has grown strong enough to belong in the claims-vs-data conversation, even though it isn't an approved indication. The platform-drug story is larger than the weight-loss story that made semaglutide famous.

the semaglutide literature is now widening beyond weight alone into organ protection. newer 2026 review work keeps emphasizing cardio-renal benefit, slower eGFR decline, less proteinuria, and broader anti-inflammatory / mitochondrial effects. that doesn't replace the STEP story, it makes the sema story less one-dimensional.

the STEP-1 number gets quoted alone, but the program is wider. STEP-2 (Davies, Lancet 2021) tested semaglutide 2.4mg in T2D + overweight or obesity. weight loss was meaningfully smaller than STEP-1, around 9.6% vs placebo 3.4% at 68 weeks. that gap is the diabetes-population effect that keeps showing up across the class. STEP-3 (Wadden, JAMA 2021) layered intensive behavioral therapy on top of semaglutide and produced an additional ~5 percentage-point bump. STEP-5 (Garvey, Nat Med 2022) extended out to 104 weeks and was the first durability source: weight loss held at ~15% at two years with continued dosing. STEP-TEENS (Weghuber, NEJM 2022) drove the adolescent label expansion at -16.1% vs placebo +0.6% in 12-17-year-olds.

trial-clinic weight loss is one number. clinic-floor weight loss is another. Rodriguez (JAMA Intern Med 2024) used electronic-health-record cohorts to compare semaglutide and tirzepatide in actual U.S. clinical practice, and the gap that shows up everywhere shows up here too: real-world tirzepatide produced larger weight loss than semaglutide, both produced less than the published trials, and dose-titration / discontinuation rates affected the outcome materially. take 'STEP-1 14.9%' as the trial ceiling, expect less in practice, and expect tirzepatide ahead of semaglutide in head-to-head real-world cohorts the same way SURMOUNT-5 showed in trial.

SURMOUNT-5 changed everything. Before that trial, semaglutide was the default obesity drug: more familiar, more prescribed, more brand pull.

SURMOUNT-5 read out in late 2024: tirzepatide 20.2% vs semaglutide 13.7% at 72 weeks. Prescribers started defaulting to tirz for aggressive weight loss. Consumers asked for Zepbound by name. By Q3 2025, Zepbound was the new standard.

Lilly went aggressive on direct-to-consumer. LillyDirect launched January 2024 as a pharmacy channel Lilly owns, selling Zepbound at $299-$449/mo by dose, bypassing PBMs. Telehealth partners (Ro, Hims, LifeMD) integrate with it. Walmart added Zepbound at LillyDirect pricing in 2025.

This is unusual for big pharma. Most drugs flow through PBMs who extract rebates and dictate formularies. Lilly chose to disintermediate. By late 2025, 35% of Zepbound scripts were cash-pay.

CVS Caremark removed Zepbound from its standard formulary on July 1, 2025, steering patients to Wegovy. Lilly called it rebate-driven, not evidence-based. A class-action followed, alleging forced switching.

About 1 in 10 Zepbound users on CVS-administered plans switched GLP-1s. Lilly said the financial impact was minimal: the cash-pay channel absorbed most of it.

GLP-1 receptor activity is familiar from semaglutide: slows gastric emptying, boosts insulin, suppresses glucagon, engages CNS satiety. GIP receptor activity adds insulin sensitivity in adipose tissue, a different pull on food preferences (fats and high-palatability foods), and possibly a lower nausea ceiling at equivalent weight loss. The two pathways synergize rather than just stack.

Lilly has been aggressive on label expansion. Zepbound is FDA-approved for obstructive sleep apnea in obese patients (2024). TOGETHER-PsO (Feb 2026) showed Zepbound + Taltz beat Taltz alone for psoriasis + obesity, with 27.1% reaching complete skin clearance on the combo.

Lilly is pushing into cardiovascular, NASH/MASH, osteoarthritis, and possibly neurodegenerative disease. The working thesis: obesity treatment fixes a primary driver of 200+ associated diseases.

GI sides are near-identical to semaglutide in prevalence. Nausea, diarrhea, constipation, 50-70% range depending on dose. The titration schedule (2.5 → 5 → 7.5 → 10 → 12.5 → 15 mg weekly over 5 months) exists specifically to manage this. Discontinuation rates at top doses mostly GI-driven.

Same as semaglutide. Stops working when you stop taking it. SURMOUNT-4 placebo arm regained 14% of body weight within a year. This is a long-term drug, possibly lifelong, not a cure. The pricing fights exist because most users will be on it for years, not weeks.
Aggregated from r/tirzepatide, r/Zepbound, r/Mounjaro, various forums 2022-2026:

As of 2026, tirz wins on weight-loss magnitude and direct-to-consumer reach via LillyDirect. Sema still owns the clearest obesity-plus-established-CV-disease story through SELECT. SURPASS-CVOT gives tirzepatide agent-level cardiovascular reassurance in T2D with ASCVD.

If you want a number, write Zepbound. If you want established CV-label coverage, write Wegovy.

The original framing captured weight-loss magnitude. The second-pass literature adds breadth. SURMOUNT-OSA showed tirzepatide materially improves obstructive sleep apnea in obesity, disease modification, not just appetite suppression.

Patient-experience data from the same program adds lived-function context past the scale. And emerging literature on GLP-1-class effects on reward biology and alcohol interoception suggests tirzepatide is hitting pathways outside metabolism. It is no longer just the bigger sema.

tirzepatide is starting to get written about as a cardiovascular-risk drug, not just the most potent scale drug in the class. a 2026 network meta-analysis found the class-level CV signal remains strong, with tirzepatide comparable to individual GLP-1 agents at the agent level. the conversation is shifting from raw weight loss toward total risk reduction.

Two of the three mechanisms are familiar from tirz: incretin-driven satiety and insulin secretion. The third, glucagon receptor agonism, raises resting energy expenditure measurably, accelerates hepatic fat oxidation, and keeps weight loss running where dual-agonists plateau.

The trade-off: triple agonism also amplifies autonomic side effects and introduces a novel neurological signal (dysesthesia) not seen with sema or tirz.
This is where retatrutide starts paying for its efficacy. Standard GLP-1 GI sides present at rates similar to tirz. But reta introduces side effects not prominent in sema/tirz:

Phase 2 MASLD/MASH data showed >80% relative liver fat reduction at 12mg. Sema reaches ~30%. Tirz closer to 50%. The glucagon pathway drives hepatic fat oxidation directly. This may be reta's strongest cardiometabolic benefit beyond the weight itself. Phase III MASH data is pending.

TRIUMPH-4 enrolled patients with obesity plus knee osteoarthritis. The trial showed not just weight loss but a 75.8% improvement in WOMAC pain scores, larger than most dedicated OA drugs produce. Some of this is mechanical (less weight on joints). But the effect size suggests reta may have direct anti-inflammatory activity. A knee-OA label would expand the addressable market well beyond obesity pharma.
Reta has been widely used in the gray market since late 2023. Self-reports are consistent with phase II/III data in important ways:

Retatrutide is not FDA-approved as of May 2026. Gray-market access is widespread. The tradeoffs are real: no COA confirmation, no manufacturing controls, no supervised titration for a drug with a roughly 21% rate of a novel neurological side effect at the 12 mg dose in TRIUMPH-4. The clinical program is the evidence. The gray market is not.

Reta's edge over semaglutide and tirzepatide comes from the glucagon arm. The third receptor is doing real work, not riding on GLP-1 and GIP. Current mechanistic reviews lay out cleanly why triple agonism produces more weight loss than dual-incretin drugs: glucagon activation adds an energy-expenditure component the other two don't provide.

The ~24% body weight loss in phase 2 isn't a fluke of the patient population. It's what the pharmacology predicts. Tolerability questions (dysesthesia, heart-rate elevation, hepatic effects) remain under phase 3 evaluation and will shape the final label. The core efficacy thesis has held up across every readout so far.

retatrutide still reads like the future arriving faster than the evidence can mature. the newer literature treats it as a systems-pharmacology shift and an access problem as much as a drug story. people are debating its impact before phase III has finished the boring work. the ceiling still looks enormous. the formal proof is still not done.

Same GLP-1 receptor agonism as semaglutide: slows gastric emptying, stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppresses glucagon, acts on CNS satiety centers. The mechanism didn't change between lira and sema. The pharmacokinetics did. Daily injection vs weekly. Sema sits on the receptor longer at higher average concentration. That's the whole advantage.

Nearly identical GI profile to semaglutide: nausea, diarrhea, constipation, vomiting in 40-75% of users depending on dose. Titration schedule (0.6 → 1.2 → 1.8 for T2D, up to 3.0 for obesity) manages tolerability. Same class warnings: thyroid C-cell signal, rare pancreatitis, gallbladder events.

Daily injection is liraglutide's fatal flaw. Real-world adherence data shows daily GLP-1 patients miss substantially more doses than weekly patients. Once sema and tirzepatide shipped weekly, most lira patients switched within a year or two. By 2022-2023, weekly was the default. Lira is what you get when coverage, tolerability, or pediatrics says otherwise.
Liraglutide user reports are quieter than sema/tirz, partly because most lira users were early adopters who have since switched:

In adult obesity, liraglutide is the legacy GLP-1: daily dosing and 6 to 8 percent weight loss leave it behind weekly semaglutide and tirzepatide. The remaining relevance is pediatric use, insurance-driven access, cardiovascular-risk history in T2D, and a longer safety record than the newer drugs.

The adult obesity story hasn't improved. Sema and tirzepatide outclass lira on effect size and dosing.

The pediatric evidence base has improved. Trials in children 6-12 with obesity push the discussion younger than the adolescent framing. Real-world severe-adolescent data moves the picture out of tightly controlled trials and into messy clinic populations.

For adults in 2026, lira is a legacy drug that still matters in specific insurance and age brackets. For pediatrics, it's closer to first-line than most peptide-space readers realize.

liraglutide's newer papers care less about headline weight loss and more about composition: what fraction is fat, what happens to lean tissue, how quickly those shifts show up. lira is the older GLP-1 benchmark now. the question is which corners it still holds vs which the newer drugs have walked away with.

The pituitary fires growth hormone in pulses through the day, on signal from hypothalamic GHRH. Tesa mimics that signal and triggers the pulses.

The released GH hits the liver, which makes IGF-1. IGF-1 is the actual fat-mobilization signal.

Visceral fat responds, subcutaneous fat does not. GH receptors are denser in visceral adipose tissue, and the tissue is more metabolically active.
Cleaner than GLP-1s on the gut. Its own concerns, all related to elevated GH and IGF-1.

The crowd tesa has quietly captured: men 40+, often lean or only moderately overweight, with stubborn visceral fat. Muscle is decent, subcutaneous fat is fine, deep belly fat is the obsession.

Peptide clinics sell it as body recomposition. Longevity and biohacker stacks bundle it with other GH-axis compounds.

The FDA label says tesa is not for weight loss. The HIV-lipodystrophy evidence does not transfer cleanly to a 45-year-old man with a soft midsection.
Aggregated from peptide forums, men's health communities, biohacker circles, 2019-2026.

For visceral fat specifically, tesa is more selective and preserves the natural pulse rhythm. For broader recomp, joint function, or the loose bucket of "anti-aging" effects, HGH covers more ground and carries more risk.

Peptide clinics have largely settled on tesa as the cleaner option for visceral fat, and reserve HGH for patients who want the full-spectrum effect. Both elevate IGF-1. Both require monitoring.

prose.header: beyond visceral fat: liver and cognition
prose.body:
Tesa gets reduced to 'the belly-fat peptide,' which undersells the molecule. Hepatic fat reduction in HIV-lipodystrophy is a real secondary outcome. Newer transcriptomic work suggests the liver effects are active signaling, not a passive consequence of less visceral fat.

The more interesting branch: cognitive outcome data in people with HIV. Most peptide writeups skip the neurocognitive domain entirely. The HIV-lipodystrophy indication was never only about appearance.

the tesa story is branching beyond visceral fat. a 2025 phase 2 trial in virally suppressed people with HIV looked at neurocognitive impairment, consistent with the idea that fixing ectopic fat and GH signaling has downstream brain effects.

interesting, and a reminder of how niche the strongest human data still are.

GH binds receptors on nearly every cell. the primary downstream effect is liver IGF-1 production, which drives most of the systemic anabolic and growth signal. GH also acts directly on adipocytes (lipolysis), muscle protein synthesis, and bone metabolism. the endogenous hormone pulses 10-15 times per 24 hours, biggest pulse during deep sleep. injected HGH doesn't replicate that pattern. that's the central tradeoff of direct replacement.

at physiologic replacement doses in deficient patients, HGH is well-tolerated. at supraphysiologic doses in healthy adults (the off-label anti-aging and performance pattern), the side-effect risk climbs fast:

HGH sits in an unusual legal position in the US. under 21 U.S.C. § 333(e), distribution or possession with intent to distribute for any use other than FDA-approved indications "pursuant to the order of a physician" is a five-year federal felony. stricter than almost any other peptide on this site. personal use with a valid prescription stays legal. distribution for off-label performance or anti-aging use does not.

the on-label patient base, pediatric and adult GHD, is well-studied and reports consistent, expected effects. the off-label landscape is different. self-reports from anti-aging and performance use scatter widely:

if the goal is replacing a documented GH deficiency, HGH is the direct solution. if the goal is enhancing natural pulsatile GH release, GHRH analogs (sermorelin, tesamorelin, CJC-1295) sit upstream and preserve the natural rhythm. two different categories. HGH replaces the hormone. GHRH peptides ask the body to make more of its own. most peptide-clinic protocols outside true GHD have moved to the GHRH analog approach for one reason: those aren't covered by the criminal HGH statute.

prose.header: the weekly-GH shift, and what it doesn't prove
prose.body:
the formulation landscape shifted. once-weekly somatrogon now has adult and long-term pediatric data showing efficacy comparable to daily recombinant GH with much better adherence. a real win for the patients who need GH.

adherence matters more than it sounds. missed-dose drift is the single biggest driver of underperformance in daily-injection GH therapy. weekly formulations largely eliminate it. for approved deficiency indications, the 2026 treatment landscape is the best it's been.

anti-aging use in otherwise-healthy adults remains a separate conversation with a separate evidence base. modest body-composition changes in meta-analyses. not the transformation the clinics sell.

modern GH literature is less about whether replacement works and more about long-term safety at scale. KIMS matters because it moves the conversation from small endocrine cohorts to registry-level surveillance. that supports medical replacement in confirmed deficiency far better than it supports casual anti-aging use.
HCG binds and activates the LH/CG receptor (LHCGR). what happens downstream depends on the tissue:
HCG is generally well tolerated. the specific risks depend on who is using it and why:

Albert T.W. Simeons published his HCG weight-loss protocol in 1954 after watching pregnant women in India on calorie-deficient diets. his claim: HCG reprograms the hypothalamus to burn stored fat selectively, so a 500-calorie diet works without muscle loss.

controlled trials since the 1970s have consistently failed to find any fat-loss effect of HCG beyond the calorie restriction itself. the diet still circulates in commercial weight-loss programs, often sold with homeopathic drops that contain no HCG at all.
the reports split clean by use case:

March 23, 2020 is the date that matters. the FDA reclassified HCG as a biologic under BPCIA. overnight, 503A compounding pharmacies could no longer legally produce it. the compounded HCG market had been large, and many TRT clinics ran on it for affordable supply.

the shutoff funneled access into a handful of FDA-approved brands (Organon's Pregnyl, Ferring's Novarel, Serono's Ovidrel). supply has stayed uneven by product and pharmacy channel since, with allocation pressure on some brands rather than one clean nationwide shortage.

most readers know HCG as the TRT-adjunct peptide. the modern literature on fertility is where its depth actually lives. reviews of HCG in male infertility and secondary hypogonadism reinforce a central role in fertility preservation during androgen therapy.

intrauterine HCG before embryo transfer keeps being explored as more than a trigger shot. the broader uterine-infusion literature keeps the drug in circulation well past the pregnancy-test framing. the TRT-fertility bridge is real. it's just one use among several where HCG is still clinically alive.

the newer hCG literature keeps the drug in its legitimate lane: fertility preservation, hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, and selected infertility protocols. what it does not do is validate the broader wellness-clinic habit of treating hCG as a universal TRT sidecar. the update is less 'new miracle effect' and more 'the evidence is strongest when the indication is narrow.'

In the 1980s Pickart and colleagues established that GHK has a strong natural affinity for copper and readily forms the GHK-Cu chelate. the copper-bound form drives most of the biological activity. copper is a required cofactor for lysyl oxidase, which crosslinks collagen and elastin, and for several antioxidant enzymes. GHK-Cu is a targeted copper delivery system and a signaling peptide in one molecule.
GHK-Cu acts as a signal peptide and a carrier peptide at the same time. the carrier function delivers copper to enzymes that need it. the signal function is the more interesting part:

follicle biology maps cleanly onto what GHK-Cu does. it stimulates dermal papilla cell proliferation, extends anagen, shortens catagen, blunts miniaturization, and upregulates VEGF and HGF. Pyo et al. 2007 showed GHK-Cu protects dermal papilla cells from apoptosis: caspase-3 activity cut 42.7%, PARP cleavage cut 77.5%. a 2023 Chinese mouse study with an ionic-liquid microemulsion carrier beat 5% minoxidil head-to-head, follicles entering early growth at day 6 vs day 9. a 2025 JAAD International paper from Brown University tattooed minoxidil, dutasteride, and copper peptide together for androgenetic alopecia across 5 monthly sessions. promising regrowth, n=7.
among the cleanest safety profiles of any peptide:

the distinction matters. GHK-Cu has 50 years of topical evidence and maybe 5 to 10 years of serious injectable work. the topical product enters skin layers slowly, acts locally, and has an exceptional safety margin. injectable is newer. the mechanism is reasonable. the human trials are fewer. most cosmetic dermatology literature is topical. most wound-healing animal work used injectable or topical gel. peptide-clinic injectable use is ahead of its evidence.
reports divide by product type:

GHK-Cu is real. the biology is real. the mechanism is well characterized. the part to internalize is scale: topical GHK-Cu is a cosmetic-tier intervention, not a medical-tier one. equivalent to mid-tier cosmetic actives, below prescription retinoids. anyone expecting dramatic effects gets disappointed. anyone expecting subtle, consistent gains over months usually gets what they came for. one of the few peptides where the science actually supports the marketing, with the caveat that the marketing still tends to overstate the effect.

the newer GHK-Cu literature is increasingly about delivery, not just biology. a 2025 review makes the uncomfortable but important point that this peptide is hydrophilic enough that formulation quality may matter as much as the ingredient label. in other words: the molecule may be real, but the bottle still has to solve skin penetration. Ogorek et al. (Molecules 2025) mapped the liposomal-formulation lane in detail: even with lipid carriers, transdermal flux remains the limiting step. Greco et al. (Bioconjugate Chem 2025) took a different angle and published a hyaluronic-acid-GHK copper conjugate with antioxidant and angiogenic activity in cell assays. preclinical only, but evidence that material-science work on the molecule continues outside the cosmetic vertical.

LKKTETQ is the actin-binding site of thymosin beta-4. Tβ4 sequesters G-actin (the monomers) and releases them on demand for F-actin (filament) assembly. that's the cytoskeletal machinery cells use to migrate, heal, and reorganize.

TB-500 is claimed to do the same: endothelial differentiation, angiogenesis, keratinocyte migration, collagen deposition, inflammation reduction. whether a 7-aa fragment faithfully reproduces a 43-aa protein's biology is the open question. half-life is shorter. tissue distribution is probably different.
limited data. what exists looks clean. limited is the operative word:
TB-500 reports cluster around musculoskeletal recovery, the same use case as BPC-157:

frequently paired, different tissue targets. BPC-157 has stronger documentation in GI, tendon, and visceral healing. TB-500 leans toward muscle, broader soft tissue, and vascular regeneration on mechanism alone.

the "wolverine stack" bet is that combining them adds healing across tissue types. neither has controlled data vs the other or vs the combination.

if you had to pick one on evidence, BPC-157 has the deeper (still limited) research base.

the most clinically mature work in the thymosin beta-4 family isn't on the heptapeptide vendors sell as TB-500. it's on full-length Tβ4, now in legitimate clinical use for ocular surface disease, including adjunctive treatment for bacterial keratitis and neurotrophic keratopathy.

that matters for the TB-500 discussion because it establishes the family's regenerative biology as real in human disease. the recovery outcomes users report with the fragment are biologically plausible. the mechanism is active. the parent protein works clinically. the fragment keeps the core actin-binding motif.

what's unresolved is whether the exact vendor heptapeptide reproduces the full protein's effect magnitude. users who compare the two usually describe stronger effects from the full-length peptide.

the recent Tβ4 literature keeps strengthening the parent biology. angiogenesis, migration, anti-inflammatory signaling. that does not magically create a clean human TB-500 dataset.

mechanistic confidence keeps creeping up. direct clinical proof for the gray-market product stays behind.

GHRH receptor agonism in the anterior pituitary. GHRH binds its receptor on somatotroph cells, cAMP rises, PKA fires, GH synthesis and release follow. CJC-1295 hits the same receptor the same way. the four substitutions don't change receptor binding, they just keep the peptide from getting chewed up by proteases. the DAC version adds the albumin tether that turns a short-acting agonist into a long-acting one.
what shows up in phase 1 data and peptide clinic reports:
what peptide clinic and forum users say after months of use:

both are GHRH analogs. sermorelin is the original FDA-approved GHRH (1-29), discontinued commercially in 2008 but still available through compounding. half-life around 30 minutes, natural pulsatile GH release.

CJC-1295 with DAC is engineered for sustained elevation. CJC-1295 without DAC (Mod GRF 1-29) looks a lot like sermorelin in profile, with extra stability from the four substitutions.

want the natural pulse pattern: sermorelin or no-DAC CJC-1295. want maximum cumulative GH from infrequent dosing: CJC-1295 DAC, with the desensitization caveat attached.

prose.header: DAC vs no-DAC, analytics, and why the distinction matters
prose.body:
half the confusion in the CJC-1295 community comes from treating the DAC and no-DAC forms as interchangeable. they're not.

the DAC variant binds albumin and drives sustained GHRH-receptor activation over roughly a week. that is a fundamentally different pharmacology from a pulse. the no-DAC form, sold as modified GRF 1-29, clears in about 30 minutes and matches the pulsatile pattern community use claims to want.

modern analytical work tracks both forms in circulation and their distinct metabolic fingerprints, which matters for both efficacy expectations and anti-doping detection.

the pharmacology argument for no-DAC + ipamorelin is short pulses, natural patterns, predictable timing. that is still not the same as an approval-grade chronic-use evidence base.

the newer CJC literature is telling in a different way: a lot of it is antidoping metabolism and detection work rather than new human efficacy trials. that usually means a compound is better established as a performance-enhancement risk than as a modern therapeutic program. so the clinical story here still hasn't meaningfully advanced.

Ipamorelin binds GHS-R1a, the ghrelin receptor, on somatotroph cells of the anterior pituitary. The receptor activation engages G-protein signaling through the Gαq/11 subunit, which activates phospholipase C, hydrolyzes PIP2 into IP3 and DAG, releases calcium from the endoplasmic reticulum, and ultimately triggers GH vesicle release. This is a completely separate signaling pathway from GHRH-receptor activation (which uses cAMP/PKA). The separate pathways are why ipamorelin and CJC-1295 synergize, they activate the same cells via independent receptors, producing a larger GH pulse than either alone.
Ipamorelin has the cleanest side effect profile of any GH secretagogue:
Peptide clinic and gray-market reports on ipamorelin are more consistent than most peptides, the selectivity means fewer confounding side effects:

GHRP-6 raises GH and cortisol, prolactin, ACTH, plus pronounced hunger. GHRP-2 is slightly cleaner but still raises cortisol and prolactin. Hexarelin raises GH and both stress hormones, too. Ipamorelin raises GH only.

The trade-off is a lower peak. For chronic use, selectivity wins. For a one-time diagnostic GH stim, an older GHRP gives a bigger spike.

Ipamorelin's cleanest-in-class framing holds up better than the older GHRPs. It triggers GH release with less cortisol and prolactin noise, and that selectivity explains why it became the preferred clinic pairing partner. But the clinical program did not produce an approved indication: the postoperative-ileus trial was well tolerated but did not separate cleanly from placebo, and FDA has flagged route-specific safety gaps for compounded use. The pharmacology is unusually coherent for a gray-market peptide. The clinical package is still incomplete.

on March 4, 2013, the FDA formally ruled that Geref (both formulations) was NOT withdrawn for safety or effectiveness reasons. the trigger was a 2012 citizen petition from attorney Alvin J. Lorman. the practical consequence: FDA could now approve ANDAs for generic sermorelin if any manufacturer pursued the path. none have. the same ruling gave compounding pharmacies stronger legal standing, since sermorelin is a previously approved drug that was pulled for business reasons.

standard GHRH receptor agonism. sermorelin binds the GHRH receptor on pituitary somatotrophs and fires the same cAMP/PKA pathway native GHRH uses. result: GH secretion into circulation. the short half-life (~6 minutes IV) keeps the response pulsatile, not sustained. each injection produces one natural-looking pulse. by design. sustained GHRH would desensitize the receptor and trigger negative feedback.
sermorelin has a cleaner profile than HGH because the feedback loops stay intact:
user reports split into two camps: pediatric GHD patients (well-documented in trials) and adult off-label users (compounding era):

all three are GHRH analogs. sermorelin is native GHRH (1-29), ~6 min half-life, prior FDA approval. CJC-1295 no-DAC is GHRH (1-29) with 4 substitutions for protease resistance, ~30 min half-life, never FDA-approved. tesamorelin is a 44-amino-acid GHRH analog with an N-terminal trans-3-hexenoyl tail, currently FDA-approved for HIV lipodystrophy only. for pure GHRH signaling with preserved feedback, all three behave alike. sermorelin owns the historical approval. CJC-1295 no-DAC has slightly better pharmacokinetics. tesamorelin has the active FDA approval and reimbursement, but on a narrow indication.

sermorelin has something most community peptides do not: a real FDA-approval pedigree.

the 1990s pediatric GHD trials that got Geref approved showed consistent growth-velocity gains in kids with documented deficiency. clean safety profile, predictable mechanism.

modern adult GHD has moved to direct recombinant GH for efficiency. but the sermorelin pharmacology is the same one that worked in the trials. it just produces modest effects in adults without documented deficiency, which is the population clinics target.

the pediatric data supports sermorelin doing what it claims in kids who need it. adult off-label use borrows from that record without replicating the approval context.

sermorelin's recent footprint drifts toward analytical detection, not fresh treatment evidence.

that fits the current read: medically real, historically validated, no new wave of modern efficacy trials. the forward motion is regulatory and antidoping, not therapeutic.

The melanocortin system is a set of neuropeptides and receptors involved in sexual function, feeding behavior, immune response, and pigmentation. Bremelanotide non-selectively activates MC1R, MC3R, MC4R, and MC5R. The therapeutic effect comes from MC4R activation on neurons in the hypothalamus and limbic system, brain circuits involved in sexual motivation and arousal. This is mechanistically distinct from PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil), which work on peripheral vasculature. PT-141 drives desire centrally; sildenafil enables erection peripherally.
PT-141's side effect profile is well-characterized from the FDA review process:
PT-141 user reports are a mix of Vyleesi patients (in the approved indication), men using off-label for ED, and broader peptide-clinic users:

PT-141 is the refined pharmaceutical successor to melanotan II. Same receptor family (melanocortin), different molecular structures, different side effect profiles. PT-141 is a cyclic heptapeptide with FDA approval; melanotan II is a linear synthetic analog of alpha-MSH with no approval anywhere. PT-141's hyperpigmentation risk is lower at approved dosing than melanotan II's (which is used specifically for tanning). The overlap in sexual arousal effects is real; melanotan II was the starting point that led Palatin to PT-141.

Bremelanotide's Phase 3 efficacy data supported FDA approval for HSDD in premenopausal women, and the long-term safety extension that followed confirmed the tolerability profile held over extended use. The mechanism, central MC4R agonism acting on sexual-desire pathways rather than peripheral vascular effects, is genuinely different from PDE5 inhibitors and fills a treatment gap that sildenafil/tadalafil don't address. Critics of the effect size make a real argument: average efficacy is moderate, and response is heterogeneous across patients. Individual users who respond often report dramatic subjective effects; those who don't respond typically notice little. Side effects, nausea, flushing, transient blood pressure changes, occasional focal hyperpigmentation, are well-characterized across the full development program and manageable with appropriate use patterns.

post-approval bremelanotide work is still more about positioning and expectation-setting than about a dramatic efficacy upgrade. newer reviews keep it in the conversation for HSDD, but the tone is clinical and careful rather than euphoric. that's consistent with the broader read: real effect, modest ceiling, niche fit.

In 2000, Palatin Technologies, which had been developing melanotan II for sexual dysfunction after the arousal effects were noticed, formally abandoned the program in favor of the spin-off compound PT-141 (bremelanotide). Competitive Technologies, operating on behalf of the University of Arizona, licensed melanotan II to Clinuvel Pharmaceuticals in 2006 as a tanning agent. Clinuvel ran into regulatory resistance, health authorities were not keen on approving a drug that facilitated tanning, and shifted focus to Melanotan I (afamelanotide) for rare skin disorders. Clinuvel's afamelanotide received FDA approval in 2019 for erythropoietic protoporphyria, a different indication. Melanotan II itself remains without commercial approval anywhere.
Non-selective melanocortin receptor agonism, melanotan II activates all five receptor subtypes. The effects map to where the receptors are:
Melanotan II has a broader side effect profile than PT-141 due to its less-refined receptor selectivity and the dosing typical in tanning applications:
MT2 user reports are more extreme than most peptides in this tier, the effects are substantial and sometimes overwhelming:

These are different compounds. Melanotan I (afamelanotide) is a 13-amino-acid linear analog of α-MSH with relative MC1R selectivity. It's FDA-approved as Scenesse (Clinuvel Pharmaceuticals) for erythropoietic protoporphyria. Melanotan II is a 7-amino-acid cyclic heptapeptide, non-selective for MC1-5R, never approved anywhere. MT1 has stronger tanning selectivity and fewer non-pigmentary effects. MT2 has broader effects (appetite, libido, skin) and worse side effect profile. They often get conflated in gray-market marketing but they're pharmacologically distinct.

prose.header: both sides of the ledger keep filling in
prose.body: Melanotan II does what users buy it for, the tanning effect is reliable, and melanocortin-receptor pharmacology is well-characterized enough that effect and mechanism both have real literature behind them. The newer work adds to both sides of the entry simultaneously. On the efficacy side, MT-II effects on thermogenesis and brown-fat signaling add dimensions beyond pigmentation. On the risk side, oral mucosa pigment changes have been documented alongside the already-known skin pigmentation effects, and priapism case reports in men continue to accumulate. The community-use pattern, intermittent dosing to develop and maintain a tan, produces real results and real side effects in a ratio that depends heavily on individual susceptibility and dosing discipline. The compound works; the side effect profile is genuine and occasionally serious.

the update here is mostly public-health, not performance. a 2026 dermatology paper focuses on how unregulated melanotan use is being promoted through social media and frames the risk as both dermatologic and regulatory. that fits the broader pattern: attention is moving toward what the market is doing with MT-II, not toward turning it into a legitimate medicine.
The theoretical rationale is multi-pathway coverage:
KLOW inherits the side effect profile of its components. Aggregating the individual profiles:
KLOW reports from peptide clinic and gray-market users:

GLOW is the three-peptide version of KLOW: GHK-Cu + BPC-157 + TB-500, without the KPV. Typical ratio 50/10/10 (GHK-Cu dominant). The difference is KLOW adds KPV specifically for gut/mucosal inflammation support. GLOW is focused on tissue repair + cosmetic effect; KLOW adds an anti-inflammatory/antimicrobial layer. Both emerged from the same peptide-clinic culture at roughly the same time. Neither has formal research.

KLOW's four components each have independent research bases that justify their inclusion. KPV has consistent anti-inflammatory data across colitis models, with newer hydrogel-delivery work extending the mechanism into more practical formulations. GHK-Cu continues to accumulate delivery-science literature that matters for actual tissue-level exposure. BPC-157 and TB-500 have the preclinical healing footprints that established them individually. The blend's clinical logic, combining localized cytoprotection, mucosal anti-inflammatory activity, copper-peptide remodeling, and systemic tissue repair, is coherent and reflects how peptide clinics actually think about multi-mechanism healing protocols. Community response to the blend is consistently positive in the recovery-and-aesthetic use case it targets. The evidence gap that remains is blend-specific trial data, which no one is incentivized to produce for a compounded product that doesn't have proprietary-drug economics behind it.

the ingredient-level evidence keeps moving, but the blend itself is still extrapolation. newer BPC work continues to favor tendon, ligament, muscle, and junctional repair, which helps the logic of the formula. what still doesn't exist is a controlled paper on this exact four-part stack.
The theory mirrors KLOW minus one mechanism:
GLOW inherits the individually-clean profiles of its components:
GLOW user reports skew cosmetic, consistent with the marketing positioning:

For skin-quality and cosmetic claims, GHK-Cu alone has the stronger evidence base. For mechanical-injury claims, BPC-157 + TB-500 maps more directly to the claimed use case. GLOW combines all three for breadth rather than depth in any one area. Whether the breadth is worth the cost and complexity over sequential single-peptide use is an honest open question that no trial has answered.

prose.header: GHK-Cu is doing most of the work
prose.body: GLOW's three-component formulation is anchored by GHK-Cu, which has the strongest evidence base of the three and continues to benefit from active delivery-science research. Cu-GHK hydrogel wound-healing materials and ionic-liquid microemulsion formulations for hair regeneration represent real progress in getting the peptide to target tissue, the limitation that undercuts most cosmetic copper-peptide products. BPC-157 and TB-500 broaden the use case rather than elevate the proof standard, adding localized cytoprotection and systemic tissue repair to the dermatologic focus that GHK-Cu drives. The aesthetic-forward positioning of the blend (vs KLOW's gut-and-inflammation focus) maps cleanly onto the component science. For skin quality and recovery, the formulation logic is solid; blend-specific trials remain absent but the GHK-Cu half carries genuine evidence.

the newer literature helps the logic of glow more than it validates the exact formula. copper-peptide work keeps emphasizing that delivery and skin permeation are the make-or-break variables, which is important for any cosmetic blend built around GHK-style signaling. the formula makes mechanistic sense; the blend itself is still untested.

The CJC+Ipa blend is two peptides in one vial: CJC-1295 (a GHRH analog) and ipamorelin (a growth hormone secretagogue that mimics ghrelin). It is one of the most common peptide-clinic stacks, but it is still a clinic formulation, not an FDA-reviewed fixed-dose product. Ratios, source quality, and CJC form matter.

CJC-1295 and ipamorelin hit different receptors. CJC-1295 activates the GHRH receptor on pituitary somatotrophs (the cells that release growth hormone). Ipamorelin activates the ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a) on the same cells. Stimulating both receptors simultaneously produces more growth hormone than either alone, 3 to 5 times more, consistently, in both published pharmacology studies and clinical observation. This isn't a theoretical synergy story. It's how receptor-pair pharmacology works.

CJC-1295 was developed by ConjuChem (a Canadian biotech in the early 2000s) with the goal of FDA approval for adult growth hormone deficiency. Ipamorelin was developed by Novo Nordisk in 1998 and advanced through various pharmaceutical programs. Neither reached FDA approval on their own commercial trajectory, not because they didn't work, but because the economics didn't pencil out for unpatentable peptide compounds. U.S. compounding pharmacies paired them in the 2010s, recognizing that GHRH-plus-GHRP pharmacology would produce a clean single-injection protocol. That's the origin story: pharma built the components, the clinical side of medicine figured out how to combine them.

The user outcome pattern is consistent enough to matter, but it is still community and clinic follow-up, not a controlled trial. The most commonly cited effects are improved sleep depth, training recovery, and modest body-composition changes over weeks to months. Side effects are usually mild in reports: transient numbness or tingling, mild water retention, injection-site soreness, and occasional hunger from the ghrelin-receptor activity of ipamorelin. The consistency of this pattern is part of the argument for the stack, but the RCT gap remains the RCT gap.

Both components are A-tier. The combination's pharmacology is established. User outcomes are consistent enough to matter, but the blend itself has no formal RCT. S-tier in this taxonomy is reserved for compounds with drug-grade evidence: FDA approvals or phase 3 trial packages. CJC+Ipa has strong component pharmacology and consistent clinic/community reports, but not drug-grade outcome evidence for the fixed blend. If a trial ever ran and came back clean, this moves to S. Until then, A honestly reflects: plausible, widely used, still under-proven as a finished product.

prose.header: blend verification and why it matters
prose.body: The pharmacology behind the CJC-1295 + ipamorelin pairing is well-supported: hitting GHRH and ghrelin receptors simultaneously produces synergistic GH pulses that better mimic physiological patterns than either peptide alone. What the modern literature adds is analytical infrastructure, detection methods for CJC metabolites, ipamorelin PK profiles, and fragmentation analysis techniques that researchers use to verify what's actually in a compound vial. This matters practically: blend vials from different sources vary meaningfully in ratios and stability, and users who've switched between vendors often report different experiences with the same nominal product. The pharmacology works when the product matches the label. Quality control is the variable most users don't think about until they encounter a batch that underperforms.

the recent literature around this stack still hasn't produced a direct blend trial. what has appeared instead is more antidoping metabolism work on the GHRH side, which tells you the compound family is active and detectable but not newly validated therapeutically. this remains a practical clinic stack, not a studied fixed-dose product.

The BPC+TB blend, usually called the Wolverine Stack, is two peptides in one vial: BPC-157 (a pentadecapeptide from gastric-juice research) and a component sold as TB-500. The name made the stack legible to people who would never read two mechanism pages first. That adoption curve is real. The trial record on the blend is not.

BPC-157 and TB-500 are paired because the mechanisms sound complementary. BPC-157 is framed around angiogenesis, growth-factor signaling, nitric oxide, and local tissue models. TB-500 borrows from thymosin beta-4 actin and migration biology. The pairing makes biological sense. It still has to be separated from claims that a fixed blend repairs human tendon injuries on schedule.

BPC-157 came from Professor Predrag Sikirić's laboratory at the University of Zagreb in the early 1990s, the first PubMed entries are from 1992. The TB-500 side is tangled with thymosin beta-4 repair literature, RegeneRx's full-length Tβ4 programs, and the later research-peptide market. The 'Wolverine Stack' emerged from the biohacking and injured-athlete communities around 2018-2020, through podcast mentions and a wave of people looking for something beyond rehab plateaus.

The stack is most commonly discussed around tendinopathy (rotator cuff, Achilles, lateral epicondylitis), ligament strains, post-procedure recovery narratives, plantar fasciitis, and persistent soft-tissue pain. The reported pattern is reduction in pain and return of function, but the reports are not blinded, controlled, or separated cleanly from rehab, rest, time, and expectation.

Pre-mixed Wolverine blend vials are widely sold on the research-peptide market. Like any blend, they carry more quality-control problems than single peptides: differential degradation rates, potential ratio drift, harder identity verification, and the added TB-500 naming problem. A label saying BPC+TB does not prove the component identity, ratio, sterility, or stability.

Both components have strong enough stories to make the pairing plausible. The strongest case for A-tier is coherence: BPC-157 has unusually dense preclinical repair literature and TB-500 points at a different repair pathway. The reason it cannot be S-tier is simple: no FDA approval, no phase 3 package, no controlled human blend trial, and no clean route-specific safety map.

B-tier would undersell the component biology. BPC-157 has one of the densest preclinical repair packages in the catalog, and the TB-500 side points at a real actin-and-migration family. A-tier here means strong mechanism plus persistent signal. It does not mean the blend has been clinically proven.

Reported side effects are usually mild in community discussion: injection-site irritation, occasional nausea, fatigue, and flu-like reports. The missing part is the formal one. No blend-specific adverse-event dataset maps identity, sterility, route, dose, cancer-context risk, or long-term outcomes. Serious-sounding certainty does not belong here.

The Wolverine Stack's pharmacology is coherent: BPC-157 drives localized angiogenesis and cytoprotection at the injury site, while thymosin beta-4 (the parent protein behind TB-500) supports broader cell migration and tissue remodeling. The 2025 BPC-157 review adds weight to the BPC half of the blend by reframing the compound as a serious therapy-plus-safety candidate, and the ocular-translation literature on full-length Tβ4 establishes that the thymosin biology behind TB-500 has genuine clinical traction. Where the evidence is thinnest isn't mechanism. It's the specific blend claim. No controlled study has tested the combination itself, only the individual components. Community reports strongly favor the combination over either peptide alone for tendon and joint recovery, which is what drives its continued use.

the newer literature still supports why people pair these two: BPC keeps looking strongest around connective tissue and junctional healing, while thymosin-β4 biology continues to support migration and repair. what it doesn't do is validate the one-vial combo as its own studied intervention. the blend remains a mechanistic stack rather than a directly tested therapy.

Semax is a synthetic heptapeptide (seven amino acids) based on the ACTH(4-10) fragment of adrenocorticotropic hormone, with a C-terminal Pro-Gly-Pro extension added for stability. The name is a contraction of 'seven amino acids' (Russian: СЕМь АминоКиСлот). Developed in Russia as a nootropic and neuroprotective peptide, approved there for clinical use in ischemic stroke, cognitive disorders, and optic nerve atrophy.

Full ACTH is a stress hormone that triggers cortisol release from the adrenal cortex. Russian researchers in the 1970s noticed that ACTH's effects on cognition and learning survived in shorter fragments, while the adrenal-stimulating effects required the longer 1-24 sequence. The ACTH(4-10) middle kept the nootropic properties without the hormonal activity. The catch: it degraded in plasma within minutes. The Pro-Gly-Pro tail fixed that and stretched the duration from minutes to hours.

Semax came out of the Institute of Molecular Genetics (IMG) at the Russian Academy of Sciences in the 1980s. Academician Nikolai Myasoedov ran it at IMG with Professor Igor Ashmarin at Moscow State University. First published 1991. The same team later built Selank with the same Pro-Gly-Pro trick. IMG is still the center of Russian cognitive-peptide research.

FDA says it intends to consult the Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee in July 2026 regarding Semax acetate and Semax free base. The proposed compounding-use questions are cerebral ischemia, migraine, and trigeminal neuralgia. That is a compounding-status review, not a drug-approval review. A favorable outcome would change how prescription compounding is treated in the U.S.; it would not make Semax FDA-approved.

Western community use is almost entirely intranasal. Reports cluster around sharper focus, more mental stamina, faster recall, less fatigue. People describe the effect as noticeable early and steadier after repeated use. Side effects are usually mild: occasional nasal irritation, transient fatigue. It does not look like the dependency or tolerance profile of stimulants or benzodiazepines, though Western long-term data is still thin.

Semax has more human clinical evidence than most 'nootropic peptides' by a significant margin. The Russian regulatory approval is based on controlled trials, the BDNF mechanism is confirmed, the safety profile is clean. Held back from A-tier by the specific constraint that most primary evidence is in Russian-language journals under a regulatory framework Western clinicians don't recognize. A favorable FDA compounding-status decision would improve the U.S. access story, but it would still leave Semax outside FDA-approved drug territory.

C-tier is for compounds with thin evidence and mostly anecdotal support. Semax clearly clears that bar, real clinical trials, real regulatory approval in at least two countries, decades of documented clinical use. The question is whether the evidence crosses into Western-accepted standards, which it does partially (mechanism studies are widely published in English) but not fully (most clinical trials remain in Russian literature).

prose.header: the mechanism keeps looking more serious
prose.body: Western readers have historically seen Semax as Russian peptide mystique because most of the primary literature was Russian-language. The recent English-language work changes that picture. Human neuroimaging shows Semax affects the default mode network, the brain system associated with attention, memory consolidation, and self-referential thought, in measurable ways that go beyond subjective report. Post-ischemic gene expression studies document specific transcriptomic changes in models relevant to stroke recovery, which is the approved Russian indication. These findings don't transform Semax into an FDA-approved drug, but they do move the compound's mechanistic story from folklore into imaging and molecular biology that's hard to dismiss. The community use pattern, intranasal dosing for focus and mental resilience, has more mechanistic support than most nootropics with that reputation.

the semax literature is still preclinical, but it isn't dead. a 2025 study linked semax to functional recovery after spinal cord injury through μ-opioid-receptor-linked signaling, which is a very different level of mechanistic specificity than older 'it seems neuroprotective' claims. still animals and cells, still no modern therapeutic trial, but at least the biology is getting sharper.

selank is a synthetic heptapeptide derived from tuftsin, a naturally occurring immune peptide, with a stabilizing Pro-Gly-Pro tail. built in Russia as an anxiolytic alternative to benzodiazepines. approved there for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) and neurasthenia. dosed as a 0.15% intranasal spray.

tuftsin is a tetrapeptide (Thr-Lys-Pro-Arg) the body cleaves from immunoglobulin G. Victor Najjar discovered it at Tufts in the 1970s and characterized it as an immune modulator that boosts phagocytic activity of macrophages and neutrophils. russian researchers noticed it also produced cognitive and mood effects in animals.

the problem: tuftsin clears in minutes. adding the Pro-Gly-Pro tail (same trick as Semax) extended duration and, unexpectedly, revealed anxiolytic activity the parent peptide did not have. the structural mod did not just stabilize. it added pharmacology.

selank came out of the Institute of Molecular Genetics (IMG) of the Russian Academy of Sciences in the late 1980s and 1990s, working with the V.V. Zakusov Institute of Pharmacology. same institutional home as Semax, same lead researchers. the clinical program ran through the 2000s and led to Russian regulatory approval in 2009 for generalized anxiety disorder and neurasthenia. it is still on Russian and Ukrainian pharmacy shelves as a prescription drug.

Western community use is typically intranasal. Reports cluster around reduced baseline anxiety without sedation, better focus under stress, and less emotional overreaction. Onset is often described as subtle rather than dramatic. Side effect reports are usually mild, occasional nasal irritation or rare transient dizziness. Dependence and withdrawal signals are not prominent in reports, but Western long-term datasets are still too thin to treat that as settled label-level safety.

Selank has stronger comparative clinical evidence than most anxiolytic peptides, head-to-head trials against benzodiazepines, documented multi-pathway mechanism, decades of Russian prescription use without major safety signals. B-tier reflects the specific constraint that this evidence sits outside Western regulatory recognition. No FDA review currently pending (unlike Semax's July 2026 review). If Western regulators eventually engage with the Russian clinical data the way they're engaging with Semax, tier placement would likely move up.

C-tier is for compounds with thin or anecdotal evidence. Selank clearly clears that bar. Russian regulatory approval came from controlled trials including direct comparison against an established benzodiazepine, that's not anecdotal, that's formal anxiolytic-class testing. The reason Selank isn't higher is the lack of Western clinical access, not any deficit in the actual evidence.

The benzodiazepine-comparison framing is real but undersells what Selank actually does. Modern mechanism work points to allosteric GABA modulation rather than direct receptor agonism, which is a meaningfully different pharmacology. That makes the lighter tolerance and dependence story plausible, but not settled at Western label strength. Selank also enhances benzodiazepine effects in chronic stress models, suggesting it can function as an adjunct rather than only a replacement. And it attenuates morphine withdrawal signs, which is relevant to the broader stress-resilience conversation the peptide is often marketed around but rarely substantiated with actual mechanism data. For users who get real anxiolytic effects without the cognitive fog of traditional medications, the Russian clinical track record and the mechanistic literature align well.

recent broad peptide reviews still place selank in the promising-but-underdeveloped bucket from a Western clinical perspective. that matters because the internet treats selank like a mature anxiolytic when the modern accessible record still looks like Russian clinical use plus mechanism work, not a Western psychiatric-drug package. the story has not upgraded nearly as much as the hype has.

two peptides in one weekly subcutaneous injection: semaglutide (the GLP-1 agonist sold as Ozempic and Wegovy) and cagrilintide (a long-acting amylin analog). both at 2.4 mg. dual-chamber pen, mixed just before injection. the first GLP-1 + amylin fixed-dose combination in history. novo filed the NDA on december 18, 2025.

GLP-1 and amylin control appetite through different pathways. semaglutide slows gastric emptying and kills post-meal hunger via the hindbrain. cagrilintide binds calcitonin and amylin receptors in the area postrema and reinforces meal satiety. hitting both at once should beat either alone, and in REDEFINE-1 it did. cagrisema 22.7% beat semaglutide 16.1% and cagrilintide 11.8% over the same 68 weeks.

novo ran a staged phase-3 program spanning 4,600+ patients. REDEFINE-1: cagrisema vs placebo vs each monotherapy in 3,417 adults with obesity, no diabetes. REDEFINE-2: cagrisema vs placebo in 1,206 patients with type-2 diabetes. REDEFINE-4: cagrisema vs tirzepatide 15 mg in 809 patients over 84 weeks. REDEFINE-3 is the ongoing cardiovascular outcomes trial. REDEFINE-11 is testing longer duration and higher-dose escalation to chase the 25%. results at ADA 2025 in chicago, published in NEJM, june 2025.

Novo Nordisk's internal messaging had positioned cagrisema as the drug that would hit 25% weight loss, a threshold that would have put it definitively ahead of both Wegovy (semaglutide 2.4mg) and Zepbound (tirzepatide). It came in at 22.7%. Shares dropped 7% on the announcement. Market commentary was harsh. But 22.7% is still the second-highest weight loss percentage ever achieved by an obesity drug in phase 3, behind only retatrutide. The 'miss' is more a story about stock-price expectations than clinical performance. A patient on cagrisema loses an average of 54 pounds off a 240-pound starting weight. That's bariatric-adjacent territory from a weekly shot.

REDEFINE 4 is the trial that matters for competitive positioning. Cagrisema vs tirzepatide 15mg, both as monotherapy, same duration, same patient population. Tirzepatide won by 2.5 percentage points (25.5% vs 23%). This is a real result. Cagrisema's dual-mechanism theoretical advantage didn't translate into clinical superiority over Lilly's dual-agonist. The implications are significant: tirzepatide stays the benchmark weight-loss drug. Cagrisema will be the #2 in its class when approved. Retatrutide (triple-agonist, in phase 3) may eventually reset the category entirely.

The taxonomy reserves S for FDA-approved compounds or those with phase 3 complete + decision issued. Cagrisema has the phase 3 data and the NDA filing but no approval decision yet. The pharmacology is established, the trial data is published in NEJM, the safety profile is characterized. If the FDA approves cagrisema in 2026, this moves to S on the first version released after approval. Currently B-tier because the tier reflects current regulatory status, not expected future status.

A-tier is for compounds with strong clinical use and mechanistic backing but without FDA approval. Cagrisema is in a different category. It's going to be FDA-approved or it's going to fail a filed NDA, and the probability distribution favors approval heavily given the published phase 3 data. B-tier here means 'in the final stretch of becoming an approved drug' rather than 'works well but unlikely to ever be approved.'

prose.header: filed, compared, and positioning in real time
prose.body: CagriSema's regulatory status has moved from investigational to Novo Nordisk's NDA filing in late 2025, with FDA review proceeding through 2026. The drug is now being evaluated against tirzepatide in real head-to-head data rather than compared indirectly, and early meta-analyses synthesizing the REDEFINE program with other GLP-1 comparisons support the combination strategy as genuinely novel. The amylin arm adds something dual-incretin drugs don't provide. Where the positioning gets complicated is whether CagriSema is a class-defining win or 'excellent drug, not the class winner.' The REDEFINE 4 head-to-head result is settled: tirzepatide won. The commercial story and final label will decide how much that matters. What's clear is that CagriSema is real Phase 3 medicine with real efficacy, not peptide-market speculation.

the cagrisema story now has the kind of comparative synthesis it was missing when the page was first written. a 2026 meta-analysis pits the combo directly against semaglutide monotherapy and placebo, which helps separate company narrative from pooled trial effect. it still looks strong, but the combo question is finally being analyzed as a combo question.

Kisspeptin-10 is the 10-amino-acid active C-terminal fragment of kisspeptin, a natural neuropeptide hormone produced in the hypothalamus. It is the short form that preserves the receptor-activating C-terminal sequence. The strongest human HSDD and IVF evidence, however, used kisspeptin-54. That makes this page a kisspeptin-pathway entry with a clear molecule caveat: KP-10 is adjacent to the clinical record, not identical to it.

Kisspeptin was discovered in 1996 at Pennsylvania State University's Hershey Medical Center as a metastasis suppressor gene. The gene was named KISS1 after Hershey Kisses chocolates, and the peptide product got the same name by extension. The sexual/reproductive function of kisspeptin wasn't recognized until 2003, when researchers demonstrated that humans with inactivating mutations in the kisspeptin receptor (KISS1R) failed to undergo puberty. This reframed kisspeptin as the master upstream trigger of the entire reproductive axis.

Kisspeptin's clinical development in sexual medicine has been almost entirely driven by one research program: Prof. Waljit Dhillo and colleagues at Imperial College London, funded by the NIHR Imperial Biomedical Research Centre and the Medical Research Council. The program has run since approximately 2015 and has produced phase 1 safety work, fertility applications, IVF trigger studies, and the HSDD trials that drew mainstream attention in JAMA Network Open. This is serious academic clinical medicine, with Imperial as the hub. The headline molecule in those trials was kisspeptin-54.

Separate from the HSDD work, kisspeptin-54 has shown striking results as an alternative IVF trigger. Standard IVF protocols use hCG to mature eggs before retrieval, which carries a significant risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS), a potentially serious complication. Imperial's kisspeptin trials demonstrated that kisspeptin-triggered egg maturation works at comparable or better efficacy than hCG but with ovarian volumes approximately 3x smaller post-trigger, dramatically reducing OHSS risk. Research has also demonstrated restoration of menstrual function in women with hypothalamic amenorrhea. Both applications suggest kisspeptin's therapeutic value extends well beyond HSDD.

Outside the clinical trial context, community use of kisspeptin-10 is mostly discussed around libido, arousal, erectile response in men, and general sexual-wellness changes. Those reports do not map cleanly to the IV KP-54 trials that anchor the strongest human evidence. Side effects described in community reports are usually transient flushing, warmth, or mild local irritation. No pattern of serious adverse events has emerged in reports, but KP-10 market use remains a different evidence lane from Imperial's KP-54 clinical program.

A-tier would require either phase 3 clinical data or equivalently strong real-world clinical use at scale. The kisspeptin pathway has excellent phase 1-2 data from one research center, but the scale is small, the regulatory pipeline is still early, and KP-10 is not the molecule used in the strongest HSDD and IVF trials. If Imperial or another center advances a kisspeptin therapeutic through phase 3 and files with a regulator, A-tier or higher would be warranted. That has not happened yet.

C-tier is for compounds with thin or mostly anecdotal evidence. The kisspeptin pathway has actual RCTs published in a mainstream medical journal, with fMRI brain imaging and objective physiological measurement. That is substantially more than C-tier. The caveat is molecule identity and early-stage scale, not lack of serious research.

prose.header: fertility is the mature translation lane
prose.body: The sexual-desire brain-imaging work at Imperial College is what made kisspeptin interesting to the grey-market community, but the more mature translational lane has always been fertility. Kisspeptin is the master upstream regulator of the HPG axis, and reproductive medicine has accumulated real data on kisspeptin-54 in IVF, oocyte maturation, and treatment of hypothalamic amenorrhea. A 2022 review establishes kisspeptin's role in oocyte maturation protocols, and a 2020 trial in IVF/ICSI-treated infertile women extends the use case beyond research settings. A 2025 review frames kisspeptins as serious fertility therapeutics, master-regulator pharmacology with real clinical interest. The sexual-desire angle is exciting but early-stage; the fertility track is where the compound is closest to mainstream medical use.

the newer human kisspeptin literature is helpful because it narrows what the compound does not seem to do. a 2025 study found that a biologically active kisspeptin dose did not increase anxiety in men or women, which matters if the field wants to use it for reproductive and psychosexual disorders. that's not flashy, but it's exactly the kind of de-risking data a serious therapeutic program needs.

Oxytocin is a cyclic nonapeptide hormone, nine amino acids, with a disulfide bridge between positions 1 and 6 that creates the characteristic ring structure. Synthesized in the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary. Endogenous roles include uterine contraction during labor, milk ejection during breastfeeding, and central nervous system modulation of social cognition, pair bonding, and anxiety. FDA-approved as Pitocin for obstetric indications since 1962. Therapeutic research explores dozens of other applications.

Vincent du Vigneaud synthesized oxytocin in 1953 at Cornell University Medical College, the first peptide hormone ever chemically synthesized. The achievement won the 1955 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. This was foundational work: before du Vigneaud, hormones were known to exist but had been studied only through extracts of biological tissue. Demonstrating that you could build a functional hormone molecule-by-molecule opened the door to everything that followed in peptide pharmaceuticals. Every peptide on this list exists in some sense because of what du Vigneaud proved was possible.

Pitocin (synthetic oxytocin for IV infusion) has been standard obstetric care in the United States since FDA approval in 1962. Uses: induction of labor at term, augmentation of slow labor, and treatment of postpartum hemorrhage. This is real, FDA-approved, widely used clinical medicine. Not the interesting story for peptide-ranking purposes, but the foundational clinical credibility everything else is built on.

One of the findings that complicates oxytocin's clinical translation is how context-dependent the effects are. In men, intranasal oxytocin can sometimes enhance negative social attention (noticing threats). In women, the same dose can enhance positive social attention (noticing affiliation cues). Effects vary with starting trust levels, relationship status, individual OXTR gene polymorphisms, and situational framing. This isn't a clean 'take the drug, get the effect' pharmacology. It's a neuromodulator whose direction of effect depends substantially on who you are and what context you're in. That complexity has made clinical trial design harder and may be part of why phase 3 expansion hasn't happened.

Off-label oxytocin use described in user forums and community surveys is mostly intranasal. Reported subjective effects cluster around reduced social anxiety, feelings of connection, and changes in emotional attunement. Effects in published research are subtle and highly context-dependent, not the 'behavioral override' marketing sometimes implies. Side effects reported in the literature are mild: transient nasal irritation, occasional fatigue, rare nausea. Theoretical concerns about chronic intranasal use downregulating endogenous oxytocin signaling have been raised in the research literature but not resolved. The evidence supports a context-dependent neuromodulator, not a daily social shortcut.

Despite being FDA-approved and having more human research than most peptides on this list, oxytocin sits at B because the interesting current clinical applications haven't translated to new approvals. The obstetric approval is settled and narrow. The off-label research has been running for 20+ years without a phase 3 success in any non-obstetric indication. This is honest: the pharmacology is real, the effects are documented, but the leap from 'modulates social cognition in controlled studies' to 'treats a chronic condition in daily clinical use' has been harder than the early research suggested.

C-tier is for compounds with thin evidence or mostly anecdotal support. Oxytocin is the opposite of thin, it has 70 years of clinical use, decades of peer-reviewed research, extensive mechanistic characterization. The evidence isn't thin. It just sits in an unusual configuration where the formally-approved use is narrow and the research-rich applications are off-label.

The simple 'love hormone' framing hasn't survived contact with modern research. What has emerged instead is a more interesting and more accurate picture: oxytocin modulates social cognition and emotional processing in ways that depend heavily on context, baseline state, relationship structure, and, in some studies, sex of the participant. A major lessons-learned review synthesizes years of intranasal oxytocin research and makes clear that universal prosocial effects were never what the data showed. For sexual function and parental behavior, the modern literature is more specific about which effects replicate and which don't. The compound isn't a social shortcut; it's a context amplifier that works with what's already there. For users interested in real effects rather than marketing claims, that distinction matters more than any specific result.

the new oxytocin literature keeps pushing toward dose nuance instead of blanket optimism. a 2025 systematic review on social cognition makes the field look less like 'oxytocin works' and more like 'route, dose, context, and target population decide whether anything happens at all.' that's a healthier place for this page to land.

MOTS-c (mitochondrial open reading frame of the 12S rRNA type-c) is a 16-amino-acid peptide encoded inside the mitochondrial genome rather than the cell's nuclear genome.
