Discovered in 2015 at USC's Leonard Davis School of Gerontology. Acts as a retrograde signal from mitochondria to nucleus, influencing nuclear gene expression related to metabolism, stress response, and cellular energy regulation. Functions partly as an endogenous exercise mimetic, levels rise with exercise, fall with aging.

Almost every peptide on this list is encoded in nuclear DNA, translated in the cytoplasm, then transported where it needs to act. MOTS-c is different: it's encoded in the mitochondrial genome itself, within the 12S ribosomal RNA region. Mitochondria have their own DNA (an evolutionary artifact from when they were free-living bacteria), but until MOTS-c, they weren't known to produce functional signaling peptides. Finding one reframed mitochondrial biology: these aren't just power plants. They're also regulators that send their own signals back to the rest of the cell.

MOTS-c was discovered and characterized by the laboratories of Pinchas Cohen and Changhan Lee at the USC Leonard Davis School of Gerontology in 2015. Cohen's broader research program explores mitochondrial-derived peptides (MDPs), MOTS-c is the most-studied member of this new peptide class. The research has been published in high-impact journals including Cell Metabolism and Nature Communications. This isn't fringe biology; it's a major ongoing research direction in aging biology.

Community reports cluster around endurance, recovery, meal-response, and body-composition language, but those reports are not a clinical dataset. The parent molecule has no completed human outcome trial, and the dosing conventions circulating online are not validated by a label, a phase 2 program, or a published pharmacokinetic package.

C-tier would imply weak evidence or mostly anecdotal support. MOTS-c has substantial peer-reviewed preclinical evidence from well-funded research programs at major universities, with mechanistic characterization and multiple high-impact publications. That's far past C-tier. The reason it's not higher is simply that human clinical trials haven't happened yet.

A-tier requires either phase 3 clinical evidence or equivalently strong real-world clinical use at scale. MOTS-c has neither. The academic research is excellent, and a parent-molecule phase 2a trial is finally recruiting, but no human outcome result has read out. Community use is also small enough that real-world outcome patterns have not solidified the way they have for BPC-157 or CJC+Ipa. Currently B reflects 'exceptional biology, just entering human testing.'

prose.header: the human signal is real, the therapeutic case is earlier
prose.body: MOTS-c is among the best-supported peptides for the 'exercise-induced metabolic regulator' framing. Endurance training measurably raises serum MOTS-c alongside aerobic capacity improvements, the compound moves with exercise adaptation rather than acting independently of it. Mechanistic work linking MOTS-c to age-dependent physical decline ties the peptide to the specific biology most community users are targeting. And emerging translational data (lung injury protection in cardiopulmonary bypass contexts, for example) extends the case beyond pure biomarker science into disease-modifying territory. What remains early is the therapeutic intervention story in healthy adults: exogenous MOTS-c supplementation in people without specific deficits hasn't been tested at scale. The biology is real; the dose-response-outcome case for everyday use is still being built.

MOTS-c keeps getting more interesting wherever stress metabolism and cell survival intersect. a new study tied lower human MOTS-c levels to postoperative lung injury and then backed that up with mechanistic work on glycolysis, oxidative stress, and ferroptosis. that's still a long way from a commercial peptide protocol, but it is much richer biology than generic 'mitochondrial booster' language. Kumagai et al. (iScience 2024) added a specific skeletal-muscle mechanism: MOTS-c acts via CK2 activation in muscle tissue, the first proper molecular pathway anchor for the 'muscle plus exercise plus metabolism' story the community has been building loosely for years.

SS-31 (elamipretide, also known as Bendavia and MTP-131) is a four-amino-acid cell-penetrating peptide designed to selectively target the inner mitochondrial membrane. Its alternating aromatic-cationic structure allows it to cross cell membranes, accumulate in mitochondria, and bind to cardiolipin, a phospholipid found almost exclusively in the inner mitochondrial membrane. Once bound, SS-31 stabilizes cristae structure, reduces oxidative stress, and improves ATP output in damaged or aging mitochondria. Approved by the FDA in September 2025 as Elamipretide for Barth syndrome.

Cardiolipin is a structurally unusual phospholipid, four fatty acid chains instead of the usual two, found almost exclusively in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It organizes the electron transport chain supercomplexes that produce ATP and maintains the tight cristae folding that maximizes mitochondrial surface area. When cardiolipin is damaged or dysregulated (from aging, oxidative stress, or genetic defects), the electron transport chain destabilizes, ATP output drops, and oxidative stress increases. SS-31 binds directly to cardiolipin in its damaged state, stabilizing the structure long enough for mitochondrial function to recover. This is mechanistically distinct from traditional antioxidants, which scavenge free radicals after they form, SS-31 prevents their formation upstream by stabilizing electron flow.

Barth syndrome is an X-linked genetic disease caused by mutations in the TAFAZZIN gene. Tafazzin is an enzyme that remodels cardiolipin. Without it, patients accumulate abnormal cardiolipin forms (high monolysocardiolipin to cardiolipin ratio), leading to cardiomyopathy, skeletal muscle weakness, low neutrophil counts, and delayed growth. Roughly 1,000 patients in the US. No disease-specific treatment existed before SS-31. The TAZPOWER trial (initiated at Johns Hopkins in 2017) tested elamipretide 40mg daily subcutaneous in Barth patients. The initial 12-week blinded phase didn't meet its primary endpoints, but longer follow-up supported the accelerated-approval case. FDA approval issued September 19, 2025 for adult and pediatric Barth syndrome patients weighing at least 30 kg, based on improvement in knee-extensor muscle strength as an intermediate clinical endpoint.

prose.header: why the phase 3 mixed results don't invalidate the approval
prose.body: Some commentary has noted that SS-31's clinical record includes failed trials (MMPOWER-3 in mitochondrial myopathy, some endpoints in Barth syndrome's blinded phase). That's real. It's also not unusual for mitochondrial medicine, the patient populations are heterogeneous, disease states vary widely in severity, and a drug that stabilizes cardiolipin can't help patients whose cardiolipin has already dropped below functional thresholds. The response window for SS-31 appears to be mitochondrial dysfunction with preserved-but-oxidized cardiolipin, not end-stage mitochondrial loss. The Barth approval reflects this: the open-label extension captured the population for whom SS-31 actually works, while the initial blinded phase didn't stratify for that distinction.

Mitochondrial dysfunction is implicated in cardiovascular aging, neurodegeneration, kidney decline, muscle atrophy, and general metabolic aging. SS-31's mechanism, cardiolipin stabilization on dysfunctional mitochondria, has obvious theoretical appeal across all of these. The aging research community has run preclinical studies showing SS-31 benefit in models of cognitive impairment, muscle aging, atherosclerosis, osteoarthritis, and kidney aging. Clinical trials specifically in aging populations have not been conducted at scale. Current off-label use in longevity-focused clinics is expensive, not covered by insurance, and based on mechanistic rationale plus extrapolation from the narrower approved populations.

Community and clinic use outside the Barth indication is typically subcutaneous injection, research-peptide doses in the 1-5mg range daily or near-daily, for 8-12 weeks with breaks. Most commonly used for cardiovascular concerns, post-cardiac-event recovery, mitochondrial myopathy symptoms, and general longevity/exercise capacity. Reported effects include improved exercise tolerance, reduced post-exertion fatigue, stabilized heart rate variability, and sustained energy during long workdays. Side effects consistent with trial data: mild injection site reactions, occasional headaches, rare mild fatigue during first week. No pattern of serious adverse events across community reports.

S-tier is for FDA-approved compounds with broad clinical indications and widely used treatment pathways. SS-31's FDA approval is narrow (Barth syndrome, ultra-rare disease) and the mechanism-driven broader indications haven't completed phase 3 approval. This matches sermorelin's tier situation: FDA-approved for a specific population but the active community and clinical interest is in off-label use. A-tier accurately reflects 'real FDA approval + strong mechanism + pending broader validation.'

Until September 2025, SS-31 was a B-tier candidate, excellent preclinical data, extensive phase 2/3 trials, but no approval. The FDA approval changes the tier placement. FDA-approved peptides go to A or S, not B. Same reasoning that placed sermorelin in A despite commercial production ending: FDA approval persists as a meaningful marker of clinical evidence even when the practical use case shifts.

prose.header: the approval lane is real, the community lane is speculative
prose.body: SS-31 (elamipretide) is one of the few mitochondrial-targeted peptides with real human-trial footing, and the 2025 Barth syndrome approval established it as a legitimate therapeutic for primary mitochondrial disease. Randomized dose-escalation data in primary mitochondrial myopathy supports the mechanism, cardiolipin binding stabilizes inner-membrane cristae structure and preserves ATP synthesis under metabolic stress. For people with documented mitochondrial disease, the drug does what it claims. The community use case for healthy adults targeting aging-related mitochondrial decline is not the approval population, the Barth syndrome and primary mitochondrial myopathy trials used specific patient selection, specific dosing, and specific endpoints that don't translate directly to longevity applications. The biology extrapolates reasonably; the clinical evidence does not yet support it at scale in healthy users.

the SS-31/elamipretide story is still being written in rare mitochondrial disease, not generic wellness use. a recent expanded-access newborn case in Barth syndrome is striking because it shows where the drug is being pushed hardest: severe, highly specific cardiolipin disease. that sharpens the page's framing, serious molecule, serious indication, still not a casual longevity peptide.

KPV is a tripeptide (three amino acids: lysine-proline-valine) that represents the C-terminal fragment of α-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). α-MSH is a 13-amino-acid peptide hormone with broad effects on pigmentation, appetite, and anti-inflammatory signaling. KPV isolates just the anti-inflammatory tail, stripping away the receptor-binding sequences responsible for the other effects. It is one of the shorter bioactive peptides in clinical research, and PepT1-mediated uptake has been studied in intestinal inflammation models, but human oral exposure and efficacy are still insufficiently characterized.

prose.header: why the fragment works better than the parent
prose.body: α-MSH has a tradeoff: potent anti-inflammatory effects alongside melanocortin-receptor-driven pigmentation, appetite suppression, and sexual arousal signaling. For inflammation-specific therapeutic use, those other effects are unwanted side effects. KPV eliminates them by containing only the three amino acids that drive the anti-inflammatory activity. The receptor-binding sequences for melanocortin receptors (MC1R through MC5R) aren't present in KPV, which means KPV acts anti-inflammatory without touching the pigmentation, appetite, or arousal systems. Shorter, cleaner, more selective. Elegant fragment pharmacology.

Most peptides can't survive the gastrointestinal tract. They're degraded by digestive proteases before absorption. KPV is small enough to sit in the PepT1 conversation, and animal work shows PepT1-mediated uptake in intestinal inflammation models. PepT1 expression is upregulated in inflamed intestinal tissue, which is why the route remains scientifically interesting. What is still missing is a strong human oral exposure and efficacy dataset. Oral KPV is plausible in a way many peptides are not, but it is not settled human pharmacology.

Despite extensive preclinical evidence and clear mechanism, KPV has not had the phase 3 clinical development program that would translate into a formal drug approval. The community and clinic story is mostly off-label inflammation interest built from mechanism, animal work, and practitioner reports. FDA's 2026 PCAC review may clarify the compounding lane, but it is not a drug approval and it is not permission to compound today. The economic problem is familiar: KPV is a naturally occurring fragment, hard to protect as a therapeutic, and without a commercial sponsor for phase 3 development.

One of KPV's main clinical presences is as a component of the KLOW blend (KPV + GHK-Cu + BPC-157 + TB-500). KLOW was constructed by peptide clinics as a combined anti-inflammatory + tissue-repair formulation, KPV handles the NF-κB inhibition and gut/skin inflammation, GHK-Cu drives collagen and skin repair, BPC-157 covers tissue healing, TB-500 addresses cell migration. For patients whose presentation combines inflammation and tissue damage (post-surgical recovery, chronic gut issues, complex soft-tissue injury), the combination approach is more commonly prescribed than KPV alone. GLOW drops the KPV component for cosmetic-focused use cases.

Community use of KPV is split between oral, injectable, and topical forms. Reports for gut inflammation cluster around reduced bloating, more regular stools, and less abdominal discomfort. Skin applications are discussed around eczema-type inflammation. Side effects in reports are usually mild, but there is no formal long-term human safety map and no FDA-approved route-specific product.

C-tier is for compounds with thin or anecdotal evidence. KPV has substantial peer-reviewed preclinical evidence, a well-characterized NF-κB mechanism, and documented PepT1-mediated bioavailability. That's not thin. The evidence is concentrated in preclinical work rather than human trials, but the preclinical foundation is solid.

A-tier requires human clinical evidence at either the phase 3 trial level or equivalent real-world clinical use at substantial scale. KPV doesn't have phase 3 trials, and while compounding-pharmacy use is real, the overall clinical volume is smaller than A-tier benchmarks like BPC-157 or the CJC+Ipa blend. The mechanism argues for upward mobility, the trial evidence currently doesn't support it.

prose.header: mucosal anti-inflammatory is the strongest frame
prose.body: KPV's cleanest case is as a mucosal anti-inflammatory peptide, not a general immune modulator. The colitis model work establishes the mechanism clearly, and newer hydrogel delivery research makes the application more practical by addressing gut-specific exposure. Antimicrobial effects documented across α-MSH-family peptides including KPV add a second dimension, the compound appears to combine inflammation control with pathogen resistance in epithelial contexts. What the data doesn't yet support is the 'whole-body immune reset' framing that sometimes appears in community marketing. KPV targets specific biology: mucosal surfaces, inflammatory cytokine modulation, mast-cell stabilization. Used for IBS, gut inflammation, or skin inflammation, the mechanism matches the claim. Used as a generic immune tonic, the mechanism doesn't support what's being sold.

KPV still does not suddenly have a strong modern human efficacy literature. what did move in 2026 is the official regulatory conversation: FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document says KPV was removed from category 2 because the nomination was withdrawn and that PCAC is slated to discuss KPV-related bulk substances on July 23 2026. that is not proof the peptide works and not permission to compound. it is, however, a real shift from obscure gray-market tripeptide to something FDA is formally evaluating.

NAD+ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) is a coenzyme present in every living cell. It's not a peptide. It's a dinucleotide: two nucleotides (nicotinamide and adenine) joined by a phosphate bridge. Essential for the electron transport chain that produces ATP. Also a substrate for sirtuins (longevity-regulating enzymes), PARPs (DNA repair enzymes), and CD38 (immune signaling). Appears on this list because it's clinically positioned alongside mitochondrial peptides in longevity medicine and peptide-clinic protocols, even though it's structurally different from every other compound in the B-tier.

prose.header: why it's on this list anyway
prose.body: The tier list is organized around what peptide clinics and longevity-focused researchers actually use and discuss. NAD+ and its precursors (NMN, NR) are in every longevity protocol and most peptide clinics offer IV NAD+ infusions alongside peptide therapies. Treating NAD+ as off-limits because it's technically a dinucleotide would miss a major piece of the actual clinical landscape. This entry treats NAD+ as what it is: a non-peptide cofactor occupying the same clinical space as mitochondrial peptides, with a different evidence profile that deserves honest assessment.

NAD+ levels decline with age. This has been measured across tissues (skin, muscle, liver, blood) using mass spectrometry in multiple studies. The decline is real and roughly linear, older adults have approximately half the NAD+ of young adults in many tissues. The consequences are well-characterized in mouse models: reduced mitochondrial respiration, impaired DNA repair, attenuated sirtuin function. Restoring NAD+ in aged mice reverses many of these dysfunctions. Whether the same thing happens at meaningful scale in humans is the central unresolved question of the longevity-NAD+ field.

IV NAD+ infusion originated as an addiction medicine treatment in the 1960s-70s (William Hitt, Louisiana). It was picked up by longevity and wellness medicine around 2015-2020 and now appears in most peptide/longevity clinic menus at $300-1000 per session. The clinic pitch is acute clarity, energy, reduced fatigue, better recovery, better sleep, and cognitive benefit. The clinical trial data backing those specific IV claims is thin. Most positive evidence in the NAD+ space comes from oral precursor studies, which are substantially cheaper than IV infusions. The price differential for IV isn't matched by a corresponding evidence advantage.

Community use spans oral NMN/NR supplements, subcutaneous NAD+ injection, and IV infusion. Oral precursor users most commonly report gradual changes in energy, sleep quality, and post-exercise recovery over weeks. IV users report more acute effects: mental clarity and energy within hours of infusion, with effects lasting days to weeks. Side effects across routes are generally mild in reports: flushing, mild nausea with large oral doses, injection-site reactions with subcutaneous use. IV infusions can produce acute symptoms including chest pressure, nausea, and anxiety, which is why the route deserves more caution than wellness-clinic copy usually gives it.

C-tier is for compounds with thin evidence or mostly anecdotal support. NAD+ has substantial basic science evidence (aging biology is well-documented), established precursor pharmacokinetics, and a real clinical literature on oral NMN/NR supplementation. The gap is clinical outcome evidence, not basic biology. C-tier would undersell the legitimate science.

A-tier requires human clinical evidence at either the drug-trial level or real-world clinical use at scale that's well-characterized. NAD+ has the commercial scale but the rigorous clinical evidence base is still developing. Oral precursor trials are accumulating, IV infusion trials are limited, and no NAD+ intervention has clearly demonstrated the healthspan or lifespan benefits that drive commercial interest. If the next 2-3 years of trials deliver clear outcomes, tier placement could move up.

prose.header: IV NAD+ and precursors are two different stories
prose.body: Longevity-clinic marketing often blends direct IV NAD+ with oral precursor supplementation (NR, NMN) into a single 'raise your NAD+ levels' pitch. The evidence bases for the two approaches are genuinely different. A rare pilot study on plasma and urine changes during NAD+ IV infusion gives direct pharmacokinetic data, useful because it's one of the few datasets where IV NAD+ itself was actually measured rather than inferred. The broader human-health NAD+ literature is dominated by precursor studies, which have better oral bioavailability and longer clinical development histories. Both approaches target the same biology (restoring NAD+ levels that decline with age), but the specific evidence supporting IV NAD+ protocols is thinner than supporters typically acknowledge. For users choosing between IV infusion protocols and oral precursor supplementation, the honest framing is that precursors have more clinical evidence; IV may have pharmacokinetic advantages but far less outcome data.

the better human data around 'nad boosting' is still landing on precursors and measured blood NAD changes, not on boutique IV drip claims. newer clinical work with oral MIB-626 shows blood NAD can be raised safely, which is useful, but it still doesn't prove that a clinic-style NAD infusion fixes fatigue, brain fog, or aging. Morifuji et al. (2024) ran NMN in older adults with walking-distance and sleep endpoints. a more functional set of outcomes than the usual biomarker readouts, with modest signals worth watching as the literature matures. a 2023 NR pilot in mild cognitive impairment failed to move CNS markers convincingly, which is exactly the kind of negative-CNS datapoint the field needed before nootropic claims hardened. the evidence base is improving, just not in the way the market prefers.

Epithalon (also spelled epitalon) is a synthetic tetrapeptide: alanine-glutamate-aspartate-glycine. It was developed as a simplified synthetic analog of epithalamin, a peptide extract of the pineal gland that Vladimir Khavinson's group had studied in the 1970s and 80s. The hypothesis was that the pineal gland produces peptides that regulate aging-related processes via melatonin and other signaling pathways, and that administering a synthetic version could reproduce some of those effects.

Vladimir Khavinson's institute in St. Petersburg has been the dominant source of epithalon research since its development. His group has published mechanistic studies (telomerase activation in cultured cells, effects on aging biomarkers in rodents), animal longevity studies (mouse, rat, primate), and one long-running human cohort study in elderly Russian subjects reporting reduced mortality. The work is serious and the publication record is real. The problem for evaluating the compound is that almost none of it has been independently replicated outside the original research program.

Community use centers on longevity-market cycles, typically framed as aging maintenance. The most consistent reported effects are improved sleep and subjective mood, both compatible with the compound's pineal/melatonin-adjacent origin. Reports of dramatic anti-aging effects, skin changes, energy, and lifespan language are less consistent and more susceptible to placebo. Acute side-effect reports are usually mild. The harder safety question is long-term telomerase and cancer biology. The gap between modest sleep/mood reports and telomerase-mediated lifespan marketing is where the C-tier designation sits.

B-tier requires meaningful independent evidence that the primary claimed effects are real. Epithalon's headline claims, telomerase activation and lifespan extension, rest on a single-lab literature with no Western replication. The ancillary claims (sleep, melatonin modulation) are more plausible but are also much smaller than what the compound is actually sold on. C-tier reflects that the compound is real and probably produces real (modest) effects, but the marketing significantly outpaces the evidence.

D-tier is bro-science or demonstrably failed. Epithalon isn't either. The Khavinson research program is serious scientific work by a real institution, published in real journals, with a plausible mechanistic basis. The compound isn't fake and the biology isn't ridiculous. It just hasn't earned the claims commerce has attached to it.

Epithalon's telomerase-activation biology is real, the foundational paper demonstrating telomere elongation after peptide exposure isn't in dispute. What's missing from most community discussion is the counterweight: telomerase activation isn't a one-way anti-aging switch. A rat study on colon carcinogenesis found epithalon-related increases in tumor development, and the 12-year Russian cardiovascular cohort, widely referenced as supporting longevity effects, was an observational study in a specific elderly population, not a rigorous anti-aging trial in healthy adults. The biology points in both directions at once. Serious discussion of epithalon has to include the cancer-biology tension, not just the telomere headline. For users treating it as a longevity peptide, that tension is the most important unresolved question.

recent cross-peptide reviews still mention epithalon as part of the recovery and longevity underground, but they do not upgrade it into a modern clinical therapy. that is actually the important update: the gap between attention and trial quality remains basically intact. the mythology moves faster than the data.

Pinealon is a synthetic tripeptide: glutamate-aspartate-arginine (Glu-Asp-Arg). Developed by the same Russian research group that produced epithalon, as part of Khavinson's broader 'short bioregulator' program, the idea that very small peptides derived from tissue extracts can modulate tissue-specific aging processes. Pinealon is positioned as a neuroprotective/nootropic compound, effectively the 'brain peptide' in the Khavinson lineup.

The Khavinson program's organizing idea is that short peptides act as 'bioregulators', signals that modulate tissue-specific gene expression and restore age-degraded function in the tissue they came from. Pinealon sits in this framework as a brain-tissue bioregulator. The thesis is interesting and has some supporting in vitro work, but it's a framework developed and largely defended by one research group over several decades without broad independent uptake in the Western neuroscience mainstream.

Community use of pinealon is niche, far less than epithalon, which itself is niche compared to mainstream nootropic peptides like semax. Reported effects when people do report them: subtle mood or clarity changes, nothing dramatic, highly compatible with placebo given the sparse evidence base. No serious safety signals from community use, but this reflects limited adoption as much as genuine safety data.

D-tier would be for something demonstrably fake or harmful. Pinealon is neither. The research program it comes from is legitimate, the theoretical framework is at least coherent, and short peptides do things. But between 'short peptides are interesting' and 'this specific tripeptide will protect your brain' there's a large clinical evidence gap that hasn't been filled.

Pinealon deserves more specific framing than 'memory peptide.' The mechanism literature points to oxidative-stress protection and neuronal survival under ischemic insult, ROS suppression in cell models, ischemia-recovery behavioral outcomes in aged rat studies, and protection after carotid occlusion in stroke-adjacent research. That's a different positioning from the vague cognitive-enhancement framing most community writeups use. It also maps better onto what users actually report: modest subjective clarity effects during normal use, but more noticeable benefits in recovery contexts (post-illness, post-stress, aging) where oxidative-stress load is elevated. The Russian-evidence-base caveat still applies, this isn't a Western-trialed compound, but the mechanism science is more specific than the marketing suggests.

pinealon remains in the same place after the newer review literature: biologically interesting, clinically underbuilt. it keeps showing up in broader peptide overviews as a recovery or neuroactive compound, but not as something that has earned modern controlled-trial confidence. so the page's skepticism still holds.

DSIP (delta sleep-inducing peptide) is a nine-amino-acid peptide isolated in 1977 from rabbit brain venous blood. The original experiment: Marcel Monnier's group at Basel induced delta-wave (deep) sleep in rabbits via electrical stimulation, collected the blood draining from their brains, and isolated a peptide fraction that induced similar sleep when injected into other rabbits. That fraction, purified and sequenced, became DSIP.

Most peptides on this tier list are either recent (post-2000) or had their clinical moment and either broke through or quietly died. DSIP is different. It was discovered in 1977, had its major clinical trial wave through the 1980s (sleep, stress, alcohol withdrawal), and then lingered as an area of low-intensity research interest for the next four decades. No decisive positive trial. No decisive negative trial. No convergence. The compound is still studied occasionally, still used in some research settings in Russia and Eastern Europe, and has a small but persistent grey-market community following.

Community use is almost entirely sleep-focused. Reports are bimodal: some users describe clear improvement in sleep onset and depth, others describe nothing detectable. That variability matches the old and mixed trial record. Side effects are minimal across community reports, but no modern responder framework has been established.

D-tier is bro-science or demonstrable failure. DSIP isn't either. It's a legitimately characterized peptide with a real, if inconsistent, clinical literature, and community responders who experience genuine effects. The compound is real and has real (narrow) utility. What it lacks is the consistent clinical performance that would place it higher.

B-tier requires either significant convergent clinical evidence or an unusually strong community track record. DSIP has had fifty years to accumulate the former and hasn't. The community track record is decent but not remarkable. If a modern trial program ever produced clean positive results, C-to-B re-evaluation would be straightforward, but at this point that seems unlikely.

DSIP is an unusual entry in the modern peptide space, a compound with real research history that mostly predates the current research peptide market. The classic 1980s reviews establish delta sleep-induction as the original claim, and human psychophysiological studies from that era document measurable EEG effects in small samples. Follow-up work through the late 1980s and 1990s struggled to pin the compound down consistently, which is why DSIP never became a mainstream sleep medication. That scattered history isn't nothing. It's a real but fragmented evidence base that's been frozen for three decades. The community sleep-use pattern is plausible given the old literature, but it is still 1980s-era science with no modern dosing or responder characterization.

DSIP has not received the kind of modern clinical rescue that would change its tier. recent peptide overviews still treat it as part of the experimental recovery / sleep cluster, which is another way of saying the evidence base is still mostly legacy and preclinical. the popularity is contemporary; the proof is not.

Thymosin alpha-1 is a 28 amino acid peptide originally isolated from the thymus gland. The thymus is a primary organ of immune development. It's where T cells mature, and the peptide was identified as part of the 'thymosin fraction 5' extract that Allan Goldstein's group characterized starting in the 1970s. Thymosin alpha-1 specifically was identified as one of the biologically active components and synthesized for clinical development. It is an immunomodulator that primarily affects T cell function and dendritic cell activity.

SciClone Pharmaceuticals developed and commercialized synthetic thymosin alpha-1 as Zadaxin. It's approved in approximately 35 countries, with Italy and China being the largest markets. The primary approved indication across most jurisdictions is chronic hepatitis B as an adjunct to standard antiviral therapy, with some additional approvals for hepatitis C, immunodeficiency, and certain cancer adjunct uses. Notably, multiple attempts to obtain FDA approval in the United States over the years have not succeeded, and the compound remains in research-chemical status domestically. This is unusual, most peptides with this much clinical trial infrastructure either get FDA approved or fail completely. Thymosin alpha-1 has stayed in a long regulatory middle ground.

The US peptide community almost entirely uses thymosin alpha-1 as a generic 'immune booster', for chronic fatigue, long COVID, general 'immune resilience,' frequent colds, autoimmune conditions, and a long tail of indications. Reported effects are generally subtle: improved sense of overall health, fewer colds, better recovery from illness. These effects are biologically plausible given the peptide's immunomodulatory mechanism, but the specific indications they're claimed for have minimal direct trial evidence. The gap between approved indications and community use is wide.

Community response to thymosin alpha-1 is generally positive but modest. The most consistent reports are from users with documented immune dysregulation (chronic infections, autoimmune flares, post-viral syndromes), who describe incremental improvement in symptoms over 4-8 week courses. Healthy users generally report less, which is consistent with how immunomodulators work: they don't do much if the immune system isn't dysregulated to begin with. Side effects are minimal across reports, consistent with the drug's established clinical safety profile.

If the tier list only considered the approved indications, thymosin alpha-1 would be B-tier or higher, the hepatitis B adjunct evidence is real and the drug has a genuine regulatory footprint in ~35 countries. C-tier reflects the honest reality of how the compound is used in the peptide community: broadly, for indications it has not been trialed or approved for, with effects that are modest and often indistinguishable from placebo in users without specific immune dysregulation.

D-tier would require the compound to be either fake, demonstrably non-functional, or actively dangerous. Thymosin alpha-1 is none of those. It's a real drug. It demonstrably modulates immune function, it has a clean long-term safety record, and it has clear benefits in specific validated contexts. The C-tier placement is about the mismatch between validated use and community use, not about the peptide's underlying quality.

Thymosin alpha-1 is often reduced to its hepatitis B/C approval pedigree or, more recently, its COVID-era use in some countries. The more interesting modern literature is in immuno-oncology. A comprehensive 2021 review synthesizes the full immunomodulator case, and newer combination-therapy studies document Tα1 paired with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors plus chemotherapy in ovarian cancer, or with radiotherapy plus PD-1 plus GM-CSF in broader oncology protocols. These aren't fringe applications. They're serious clinical investigations of a peptide approved abroad for decades and increasingly part of modern immune-rebalancing strategies. For community users treating Tα1 as a general immune booster, the modern research reframes it as a more targeted immunomodulator with real, indication-specific clinical uses outside the grey-market positioning.

the newer thymosin alpha-1 literature is aging into immunosenescence and resilience, not just infection or oncology adjunct use. a 2026 review explicitly frames Tα1 as a candidate for restoring immune function in older adults, which gives the compound a cleaner modern narrative. it's still not a mainstream longevity drug, but the target is getting more coherent.

Gonadorelin is synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), the same ten amino acid peptide your hypothalamus makes endogenously to control the reproductive axis. It's released in pulses every 90-120 minutes from the hypothalamus, travels a short distance to the anterior pituitary, and there stimulates the release of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which then act on testes or ovaries. It's the top of the HPG axis. Every testosterone, estrogen, and progesterone signal downstream starts with GnRH.

Historically, gonadorelin had two U.S. human-use indications from the 1980s: Lutrepulse (Ferring) for pulsatile ovulation induction in women with hypothalamic amenorrhea, and Factrel (Wyeth) for diagnostic pituitary testing, evaluating whether the pituitary gonadotrophs can mount a proper LH/FSH response to GnRH challenge. Lutrepulse used a programmable pump that released gonadorelin every 90-120 minutes, mimicking physiological pulsatile release. Factrel was a one-time diagnostic bolus. Both were narrow indications. Neither is what grey-market buyers are typically using the compound for.

TRT-community reports on gonadorelin are genuinely mixed. Users switching from HCG to gonadorelin often report reduced testicular volume retention and lower markers of intratesticular testosterone. Users who have only used gonadorelin report variable results, some maintain acceptable testicular function, others lose volume progressively. Healthy users who don't need TRT support and try gonadorelin recreationally typically report nothing detectable, which is consistent with the drug's proper use case being narrow clinical indications, not general enhancement. Side effects are generally minimal.

B-tier requires strong evidence for the use case the compound is actually being used for. Gonadorelin has strong evidence for its narrow approved uses (pulsatile ovulation induction with a pump, pituitary diagnostic testing) but weak evidence for its primary grey-market use case (TRT testicular preservation with every-other-day subcutaneous injection). The compound works well at what it's approved for. It works less well, by community report and by pharmacokinetic reasoning, at what most people buy it for.

D-tier is for bro-science or demonstrable failure. Gonadorelin isn't either. It's a genuine FDA-approved peptide with legitimate pharmacology, and even the off-label TRT use case produces some benefit for some users. It's not the best tool for that job, but it's a real tool.

prose.header: endocrine and fertility is where the evidence lives
prose.body: The grey-market positioning of gonadorelin as a TRT-adjunct accessory undersells the actual clinical base. Modern reviews of male infertility and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism place pulsatile GnRH therapy (which is what gonadorelin is) as a legitimate treatment option for specific endocrine conditions, not as an anti-aging convenience. The compound works in fertility preservation and hypogonadism management because its pharmacokinetics match the natural pulsatile GnRH pattern the body uses. Where it doesn't work as well is as an HCG replacement for TRT users: HCG's longer half-life matches the practical demands of weekly protocols better than gonadorelin's 2-4 minute half-life. Compounding-pharmacy context matters too, gonadorelin is a real prescription option in fertility and endocrine medicine, separate from the peptide-clinic TRT adjunct market.

modern fertility management papers still keep GnRH-pathway therapy in a narrow, legitimate lane: true hypogonadotropic hypogonadism and fertility restoration. that's a good correction to the way gonadorelin gets marketed online as a general endocrine tidy-up peptide. the evidence base still favors specific defects, not vague hormonal optimization.

Triptorelin is a synthetic GnRH agonist, structurally similar to endogenous gonadotropin-releasing hormone, but with a D-tryptophan substitution at position 6 that makes it resistant to the peptidases that break down native GnRH in minutes. The result is a molecule with the same receptor pharmacology as gonadorelin (the approved synthetic GnRH), but with a plasma half-life of hours rather than minutes, and depot formulations that release over months.

GnRH agonists are paradoxical drugs: at first they stimulate the pituitary to release more LH and FSH (because agonists are, by definition, receptor stimulators), and testosterone or estrogen production briefly surges. This is the 'flare.' Over roughly 2-4 weeks of continuous stimulation, the pituitary GnRH receptors downregulate, the system becomes desensitized, and LH/FSH output collapses. Sex-steroid production crashes with it. This late-phase suppression is the therapeutic goal in prostate cancer and precocious puberty. The early-phase flare is an unwanted side effect in those contexts, usually blocked with an anti-androgen or anti-estrogen. In PCT use, it's exactly the opposite: the flare is the goal, and the user stops dosing before downregulation takes hold.

The forum citation behind single-shot triptorelin PCT is narrower than the forums usually imply. Pirola, Cappelli, Delbarba and colleagues reported one 34-year-old man with anabolic-steroid-induced hypogonadotropic hypogonadism after Internet-purchased steroid use. A single 100 mcg triptorelin test dose was followed by testosterone normalization and return of energy and libido within one month. That is clinically interesting, but it is a case report. It does not validate repeated dosing, higher dosing, or routine post-cycle protocols.

Community reports on triptorelin PCT are genuinely bimodal. Some users report clean HPG recovery. Others report mixed outcomes, partial recovery, crashes, or prolonged suppression. A significant minority describe pituitary-desensitization-type symptoms, lowered libido, reduced well-being, sluggish recovery, that can persist for weeks. The drug is pharmacologically potent; forum consensus protocols often talk with more confidence than the evidence deserves.

B-tier would require the grey-market use case, PCT restart, to be backed by solid clinical evidence. It isn't. The published evidence is narrow and does not support the loose adoption pattern that dominates community use. HCG + SERM has the larger historical evidence base.

D-tier would require the compound to lack pharmacological legitimacy. Triptorelin is an FDA-approved drug with established endocrine effects, real trial evidence for its approved uses, and a real (if narrow) clinical literature supporting the PCT flare concept. The issue isn't the compound. It's the mismatch between the published PCT parameters and the community adoption patterns.

prose.header: the real drug is deeper than the PCT mythology
prose.body: Triptorelin is heavily used in legitimate medicine: prostate cancer, central precocious puberty, and, outside the U.S., additional gynecologic contexts. Modern literature keeps expanding the real-drug case. A new 3-month depot formulation extends the convenience profile, pediatric reviews of long-acting GnRH analogs synthesize decades of central-precocious-puberty experience, and prostate-cancer/LUTS context work deepens the adult oncology use case. This is where triptorelin's evidence base actually lives. The grey-market PCT use tries to harvest the initial flare for post-cycle HPG-axis restart, but it sits outside the serious medical literature. When it works, it works because the underlying pharmacology is legitimate. When it fails, it is often because the same biphasic pharmacology cuts the other way.

newer triptorelin work is getting more translational around delivery systems and patient burden, not around proving the core biology again. the microneedle ART paper is a good example: the molecule is established, so innovation is shifting toward how to give it better. that's what mature drugs look like.

Melanotan I (afamelanotide) is a synthetic 13-amino-acid analog of α-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH), the endogenous peptide that tells melanocytes to produce melanin. The substitutions at positions 4 (norleucine) and 7 (D-phenylalanine) block the enzymatic cleavage that would otherwise break down the peptide within minutes. What's left is a stable, potent MC1R agonist, the 'brown' melanocortin receptor, that stimulates eumelanin production in skin melanocytes, producing UV-independent tanning.

Erythropoietic protoporphyria is a rare genetic disorder affecting roughly 1 in 75,000-200,000 people, in which a defect in heme synthesis causes protoporphyrin IX to accumulate in skin cells. Sunlight, even brief exposure, triggers severe burning pain. Many EPP patients effectively cannot leave their homes during daylight. Clinuvel Pharmaceuticals developed afamelanotide specifically for this population, the melanin boost from the implant provides enough protection to allow normal sun exposure without the porphyrin-mediated pain reaction. The Phase 3 trial program demonstrated clear, measurable improvement in daily life for EPP patients, and the drug was approved in Europe in 2014 and by the FDA in 2019 under the Scenesse brand.

Community use is mostly cosmetic tanning outside the approved Scenesse implant context. The tanning effect is real and reliable, but the route, formulation, and monitoring are not the same as the EPP drug product. Side effects are milder than with melanotan II: less nausea (though some), no sexual arousal effect, occasional flushing, mild appetite changes. The concerns people don't talk about enough: new moles appearing during use, existing moles darkening or enlarging, and the absence of long-term follow-up data on skin cancer risk in the population actually using the compound.

B-tier would require the cosmetic use case to be backed by genuine clinical evidence, long-term safety data on repeated cycling, dermatological monitoring studies in the actual user population, established protocols for cosmetic (not EPP) use. None of that exists. What exists is a different drug, for a different disease, studied in a different way, being extrapolated onto a cosmetic use the regulatory evidence was never designed to cover.

D-tier would require the compound to fail on its own terms. It doesn't, melanotan I produces reliable cosmetic tanning, is milder than melanotan II, and has genuine regulatory legitimacy in its approved indication. The issue is specifically the gap between the approved medical use and the grey-market cosmetic use, not the compound itself.

Melanotan I is best understood as afamelanotide, an orphan-drug photoprotection therapy with real FDA approval (2019) for erythropoietic protoporphyria and growing dermatology interest beyond the EPP indication. The modern literature positions the molecule as a legitimate rare-disease drug with broader cutaneous potential under investigation, including exploratory work in vitiligo and other pigmentation disorders. The cosmetic-tanning use case that drives grey-market demand is a separate conversation: approved EPP dosing is a 16mg controlled-release implant under dermatologist supervision every 2 months, which is not what cosmetic users are doing. The drug is real and works. The grey-market use pattern (daily self-injection in healthy skin) doesn't inherit the approved-indication safety data and has its own much thinner risk characterization.

the afamelanotide literature is getting more pharmaceutical and less underground. a 2026 stability study maps degradation pathways in detail, which is exactly the kind of boring chemistry work you see when a peptide is being treated like a real drug product rather than a forum legend. that strengthens the approved-orphan-drug side of the page more than the biohacker side.

two real-world EPP registries published in 2025-2026 extend the orphan-drug evidence beyond the original trials. Homey et al. (2025) reported the German post-authorization safety and effectiveness cohort: durable phototolerance gains and a tolerability profile consistent with the Langendonk 2015 NEJM dataset, with the usual industry-context caveat. Seidl-Philipp et al. (2026) added the Austrian real-world cohort, showing the same effect direction in routine dermatology practice rather than trial-clinic conditions. neither registry changes the verdict, both narrow the gap between approved-trial evidence and what EPP patients actually experience on the implant in normal European care.

AHK-Cu is a three-amino-acid peptide, alanine, histidine, lysine, formulated with copper as a chelated complex. The histidine provides the primary copper-binding site (as it does in GHK-Cu and other copper-transport peptides). The molecule is part of a broader family of copper-binding short peptides that came out of the research following Loren Pickart's original GHK-Cu work from the 1970s. AHK-Cu was characterized as a compound with somewhat different biological targets than GHK, particularly hair follicle biology.

The dominant commercial frame for AHK-Cu is 'GHK-Cu but for hair.' This comparison is both useful and misleading. It's useful because it signals the compound class, copper-binding tripeptides with some shared chemistry. It's misleading because it implies the evidence base is comparable, and it isn't. GHK-Cu has forty years of peer-reviewed research, multiple cosmetic formulation integrations at major dermatology brands, and a well-characterized mechanism spanning wound healing, anti-inflammatory effects, and extracellular matrix remodeling. AHK-Cu has a much smaller specific research literature, mostly focused on hair follicle effects, and the formulations it appears in don't have the same pharmaceutical-grade integration pipeline behind them.

Community use is almost entirely topical, scalp application in leave-on or rinse-off formulations, sometimes combined with minoxidil, finasteride, or other hair-loss treatments. Reports are modest: slight improvements in hair thickness or density over months of use, often hard to separate from concurrent use of better-validated treatments. Users pursuing AHK-Cu as a standalone hair-loss treatment typically report minimal results. The compound reads as a cosmetic adjunct rather than a therapeutic, which is consistent with what the evidence base supports.

B-tier would require the hair-growth evidence base to be substantial, multiple well-powered clinical trials, consistent effect sizes, replication outside the originating labs. AHK-Cu's literature doesn't meet that bar. The compound produces measurable effects in small studies, but the gap between 'modest cosmetic adjunct effect' and the way AHK is sold is where the C-tier placement lives.

D-tier would require the compound to fail even basic plausibility or to be a marketing invention without real chemistry. AHK-Cu isn't that. The chemistry is real, the copper-peptide class does real things biologically, and small-study effects on hair follicle biology are genuine even if modest. The tier reflects 'real compound, overreaching marketing,' not 'fake compound.'

prose.header: scaffold-level biology, not direct product evidence
prose.body: AHK-Cu sits on a real biological scaffold, copper-peptide hair-follicle biology has decades of research behind it, and AHK-type tripeptides have documented effects in related contexts including osteoblast differentiation and follicle-stimulating properties. What's harder to find is direct human clinical evidence for AHK-Cu specifically. The supporting literature is mostly about the peptide scaffold (hair-growth-relevant copper-peptide mechanisms) and adjacent biology (VEGF signaling in androgenetic alopecia), not about AHK-Cu as the tested molecule. That means the entry rests on mechanistic plausibility extrapolated from the broader copper-peptide class rather than direct AHK-Cu trial data. For users, that distinction matters: the molecule belongs to a legitimate research lineage, but the specific claims made about AHK-Cu products run past what has been directly demonstrated.

TB-500 fragment (or TB-4 fragment) is a seven-amino-acid peptide: leucine-lysine-lysine-threonine-glutamate-threonine-glutamine (LKKTETQ). It corresponds to residues 17-23 of thymosin β4, the portion of the full 43-residue peptide that constitutes the actin-binding domain. The fragment is what you get when you take the functional core of thymosin β4 and strip away the N-terminal and C-terminal sequences that surround it.

Thymosin β4's primary biochemical role is actin sequestration. It binds monomeric G-actin and regulates the availability of actin for polymerization into F-actin filaments, which underpins cytoskeletal dynamics, cell motility, and wound healing. The LKKTETQ motif is where this actin binding happens. The argument for using just the fragment is that if actin binding is the mechanism, you only need the part that binds actin. Everything else is structural overhead. That argument is at least plausible. It's not an invented rationale. What it overlooks is that full TB-500's effects in vivo include contributions beyond actin binding alone: cellular uptake kinetics, interactions with other cytoskeletal regulators, and potentially receptor-mediated signaling that depends on the full peptide's conformation.

Community use of TB-500 frag is subcutaneous injection, typically in loading protocols similar to those used for full TB-500. Reported effects range from 'essentially equivalent to full TB-500' (a significant minority of users) to 'clearly weaker than full TB-500 at matched dosing' (a majority of comparative reports). Users who have used both compounds often describe the fragment as a cheaper option that works somewhat but not as consistently. Side effects are minimal in either version. The price advantage is real; the efficacy equivalence is where the tier placement differs from the parent compound.

B-tier would require the fragment to have a documented clinical track record reasonably comparable to full TB-500. It doesn't, the community user base is smaller, reports are more variable, and direct comparative evidence either doesn't exist or favors the full peptide. The fragment is in a weaker evidence position than what B-tier implies.

D-tier would require the fragment to be demonstrably non-functional or fraudulent. It isn't, LKKTETQ is genuinely the actin-binding core of TB-4, the in vitro pharmacology is real, and a subset of users do get results. The compound delivers some of what it claims; it just doesn't deliver it reliably enough to meet the claim of full equivalence to TB-500.

The modern literature on short thymosin beta-4 fragments is strong on identity and weak on clinical equivalence. Synthesis and characterization work pins down exactly what the vendor-sold N-terminal acetylated 17-23 fragment is, and doping-control detection studies show the fragment is identifiable in plasma after administration, so there's no question the compound is real and bioactive. What's missing is evidence that the fragment reproduces the full TB-500 effect profile. The best clinical work in the thymosin family is still on full-length Tβ4, particularly in ophthalmology, where regenerative outcomes are genuinely translated to human disease. The heptapeptide inherits biological plausibility from the parent protein's research but doesn't have its own matching clinical dataset. For users choosing between full TB-500 and the fragment, community reports consistently favor the full peptide, a preference that's consistent with where the supporting evidence actually lives.

the newer literature still revolves around full-length thymosin β4, not the fragment product people actually buy. that matters because the fragment's popularity keeps outrunning its direct evidence. the biology around the parent peptide continues to look interesting; the shortcut version remains an extrapolation.

HGH fragment 176-191 is the 16-residue C-terminal end of human growth hormone. In the full 191-amino-acid hGH protein, this region is associated with certain lipolytic effects, that is, stimulating the breakdown of stored triglycerides in fat cells. The research hypothesis was that you could isolate the fat-burning activity of growth hormone by using only this fragment, while leaving behind the rest of the molecule responsible for effects like IGF-1 elevation, insulin resistance, and tissue growth.

The research program behind this compound came primarily from Monash University in Australia, working with Metabolic Pharmaceuticals. The hypothesis was clean: GH has multiple functional domains, and a fragment corresponding to the lipolytic domain could be developed into an obesity drug that didn't have GH's metabolic downsides. To improve stability, the researchers added a tyrosine residue to the N-terminus, producing AOD9604. AOD9604 went through Phase 1 safety trials (passed), Phase 2a efficacy trials (encouraging preliminary signals), and Phase 2b efficacy trials (did not meet endpoints at tested doses). The obesity development program was essentially ended based on the Phase 2b results. AOD9604 subsequently found a second life as a cosmetic ingredient and a research compound, but never returned to serious obesity drug development.

Community use is usually sold as cutting-cycle lore. Reported effects are bimodal: some users describe meaningful additional fat loss compared to diet and training alone; others describe nothing detectable. The split is not well-correlated with source, timing, or any other obvious variable. The bimodality is probably the most telling feature of the community evidence base: it is inconsistent with a compound that reliably does what it is marketed to do.

B-tier requires evidence that the compound consistently produces its claimed effects. The AOD9604 Phase 2b trial program, the closest thing to a rigorous human trial of this compound class, did not produce that evidence. Community reports of unmodified 176-191 are bimodal rather than consistent. The compound doesn't clear the B-tier bar for either evidence type.

D-tier would require the compound to be a marketing invention without real pharmacological origins. 176-191 isn't that, it comes out of a genuine research program at Monash, has real preclinical lipolysis evidence, and produces clear effects in a subset of community users. The compound class is legitimate research; the clinical utility just didn't pan out the way the mechanism suggested it might.

The HGH 176-191 fragment class keeps producing interesting biology that doesn't translate into the fat-loss story it's marketed around. Newer work shows the fragment enhances doxorubicin efficacy in breast cancer cell lines, a finding that says the compound is bioactive but redirects the interest toward oncology adjunct research rather than bodybuilding use. Earlier translational work on oral administration and lipid metabolism supported the lipolytic-selectivity claim that motivated the AOD9604 development program. But older research on hyperglycemic effects of C-terminal hGH fragments complicates the 'clean fat-loss without GH side effects' framing, the fragment family has metabolic effects beyond simple lipolysis. Taken together, the modern additions reinforce a specific conclusion: the fragments are genuinely active molecules, just not in the way the weight-loss positioning requires.

the newer AOD-style fragment literature has drifted away from blockbuster obesity dreams and into narrower regenerative niches. a rabbit osteoarthritis model found intra-articular AOD9604 improved cartilage repair signals, which is interesting but also a reminder that this compound keeps finding preclinical side roads instead of a clean human weight-loss comeback.

Dihexa is a small-molecule angiotensin IV analog, technically not a standard peptide despite the naming. Designed for oral bioavailability and blood-brain barrier penetration. Binds and activates HGF (hepatocyte growth factor) via the c-Met receptor, driving downstream signaling that in rodent brains produces measurable synaptogenesis in hippocampal neurons.

The Harding lab's preclinical papers demonstrate real effects, dihexa increases spine density in hippocampal slices, improves memory task performance in aged and cognitively-impaired rodent models, and shows synaptogenic activity at very low concentrations. The 'seven orders of magnitude more potent than BDNF' figure is genuinely in the published literature. These findings are why the compound got attention and why Athira was founded to develop the class.

Athira Pharma (formerly M3 Biotechnology) was founded on this research program and took a related compound, fosgonimeton, formerly ATH-1017, through phase 2/3 clinical trials for Alzheimer's disease. Dihexa itself was not progressed. The later fosgonimeton readouts did not deliver the clinical win the field needed, and Athira moved away from the program after restructuring and rebranding. That sequence matters: the clinical-development trail does not validate dihexa, and it no longer cleanly validates the broader class either.

Community users report subjective cognitive enhancement, improved focus, and memory effects, typically within days of starting. These reports are uncontrolled, unblinded, and indistinguishable from placebo or the stimulant effects of concurrent stack compounds (racetams, modafinil, etc.). The effect claims that would validate dihexa specifically (measurable synaptogenesis in human brain tissue, improved performance on validated cognitive batteries under blinded conditions) are exactly what's missing.

F-tier requires the compound to be fake, a scam, or actively harmful beyond reasonable doubt. Dihexa isn't any of those. It's a real compound with real preclinical science. The tier reflects the risk-benefit honestly: unproven human benefit, specific mechanistic cancer concern, and no human clinical program for dihexa itself.

prose.header: the c-Met pathway is the whole argument
prose.body: Every serious read of the dihexa literature reinforces the same structural problem. The synaptogenesis is real, the foundational paper documenting c-Met phosphorylation and synaptic changes is solid mechanistic science. Broader reviews of brain HGF/c-Met signaling confirm the pathway is therapeutically interesting in neurological contexts. But the same pathway is one of the most thoroughly documented oncology concerns in modern cancer biology, HGF/MET dysregulation drives tumor progression and metastasis across multiple cancer types, and pharmaceutical programs have invested heavily in developing MET inhibitors for oncology treatment. That's not a footnote or speculative risk. It's the central reason this compound never advanced to casual human use. A drug that activates a validated cancer-proliferation pathway isn't a safety question with an unknown answer; it's a safety question with a known bad answer that users are choosing to ignore.

dihexa still lives where many neuropeptides live: compelling animal neuroscience and no real human program. a study in APP/PS1 mice showed rescue of cognitive impairment and synaptic markers, which keeps the c-Met / synaptogenesis story alive. but the absence of controlled human data remains the main fact, not a footnote.

SNAP-8 is an 8-amino-acid peptide that mimics the N-terminal region of the SNAP-25 protein, the same substrate botulinum toxin cleaves to prevent neurotransmitter release at the neuromuscular junction. The hypothesis: competitive inhibition of SNARE complex formation, reducing acetylcholine release, relaxing facial muscles, smoothing wrinkles. Same pathway, topical delivery, much smaller effect.

Botulinum toxin is one of the most potent biological molecules known. It enters nerve terminals, cleaves SNAP-25 proteolytically, and permanently disables that neuron's ability to form SNARE complexes until new protein is synthesized, weeks to months of effect from a single injection. SNAP-8 is a topical peptide that, if it reaches a SNARE complex at all, produces a weak, reversible, competitive binding effect. Calling both 'SNARE inhibitors' is technically accurate and misleading by omission.

User reports typically describe subtle texture improvements over 6-12 weeks of consistent daily use, often indistinguishable from improvements expected from any well-formulated moisturizer used consistently over the same period. Users expecting visible muscle-relaxation effects, the 'botox' framing, consistently report disappointment. Users with modest expectations calibrated to 'slightly smoother-looking skin texture' tend to be satisfied.

F-tier is for scams and actively harmful compounds. SNAP-8 is neither. It's a legitimate cosmetic ingredient that does something real at a magnitude smaller than the marketing implies. The pattern is specific to the cosmetic-peptide industry: plausible mechanism, manufacturer-friendly evidence, inflated claims, real-but-modest effects. The compound itself isn't the problem; the way it's sold is.

prose.header: the scaffold is stronger than the molecule
prose.body: The best evidence supporting topical SNARE-inhibitor cosmetic peptides isn't on SNAP-8 itself. It's on the broader acetyl-hexapeptide / Argireline family the compound is supposed to improve on. A 2024 review of acetyl hexapeptide-8 as a topical alternative to botulinum toxin synthesizes the actual cosmetic-clinical work, and newer serum-based studies document the anti-aging endpoints the category targets. Delivery-vehicle research highlights that topical penetration is the real bottleneck for any peptide in this class, regardless of sequence. SNAP-8 inherits biological plausibility from the scaffold but doesn't have a matching molecule-specific trial record. For users, that matters: the category produces modest effects at best, and SNAP-8 specifically hasn't demonstrated it outperforms the parent hexapeptide despite marketing claims to that effect.

the newer acetyl hexapeptide-8 literature is more sober than most marketing copy. 2025 reviews still describe it as a topical botox-like peptide, but they also keep circling the same hard questions around permeability, vehicle, and practical effect size. that makes this less of a miracle cream story and more of a formulation story.

Glutathione is a tripeptide made inside every cell: glutamate, cysteine, glycine, with an unusual gamma-peptide bond between glutamate and cysteine. It is the primary intracellular antioxidant, maintaining the reduced/oxidized balance that protects cellular components from oxidative damage. Millimolar intracellular concentrations. Low plasma concentrations. Very short plasma half-life. Real, essential, universal, and harder to turn into a consumer product than the label suggests.

A 1992 single-dose study found negligible systemic availability after a large plain oral dose, which is why oral glutathione got dismissed for years. Later evidence is less absolute: a 6-month randomized trial found daily 250 mg and 1,000 mg oral glutathione increased blood and cellular glutathione stores, and a small liposomal study reported faster marker increases plus oxidative-stress marker changes. The cleaner conclusion: acute plain oral glutathione is weak pharmacokinetically; chronic or liposomal supplementation can move biomarkers; durable clinical outcome claims remain unproven.

The use pattern is broader than skin whitening. Glutathione gets bought for antioxidant status, alcohol recovery, liver support, immune support, fertility stacks, athletic recovery, Parkinson's and neurodegeneration interest, cystic-fibrosis airway inflammation, chemotherapy-adjacent support, and general wellness IVs. The evidence does not move as one block. Cystic fibrosis has randomized trials. Parkinson's has small and mixed route-specific studies. Wellness IV has hydration, ritual, and a much thinner endpoint record. Skin-lightening is one use case, not the whole molecule.

Cystic fibrosis is the strongest non-cosmetic clinical signal: a meta-analysis of four randomized studies found improved FEV1, though other endpoints were less consistent. Parkinson's disease is biologically plausible because glutathione depletion is part of the disease literature; intranasal and IV work exists, but it has not matured into approved treatment. those signals matter. they do not validate broad wellness claims.

N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is FDA-approved as the antidote for acetaminophen overdose, where it restores hepatic glutathione pools under crisis conditions. That validates the pathway, not every supplement claim. Glutathione itself has disease-specific research and, in some countries, prescription or adjunctive uses such as chemotherapy support. Those should not be laundered into proof for hangover drips, anti-aging drips, or skin-lightening injections.

F-tier would be wrong. Glutathione is not fake, and the newer oral/liposomal data means 'destroyed in the gut, end of story' is too simple. D-tier is still fair because consumer claims outrun clinical outcomes. Biomarker increases are not the same as better health, and IV cosmetic/wellness use adds a safety and quality-control problem that the supplement shelf does not have.

The modern literature on glutathione skin-lightening lands somewhere between full hype and full dismissal, and the honest landing is far less exciting than clinic marketing suggests. A 2024 umbrella review of glutathione for skin lightening and melasma synthesizes available evidence into modest-effect conclusions. A randomized double-blind trial combining topical plus oral glutathione provides intervention-level data rather than pure review language, but still produces small, variable results. An older 'myth or evidence-based?' review remains a useful balancing source because it highlights how much of the enthusiasm around glutathione whitening outpaces the actual trial findings. The better literature supports modest pigment effects in selected contexts, not universal whitening outcomes, and none of the clinical data supports the IV-high-dose protocols that drive the safety warnings from the Philippines FDA and other regulators.

the newer glutathione literature is broader than the old oral-bioavailability debate. topical and oral dermatology reviews still land on modest effects, not dramatic whitening. the redox and tissue-repair literature keeps expanding mechanistically. the strongest frame is redox molecule first, cosmetic compound second, wellness IV product a distant third. on the safety side, Chottawornsak et al. (Dermatitis 2021) reported a case of toxic epidermal necrolysis linked to commercial skin-whitening pills containing glutathione. severe cutaneous adverse reaction, real published harm, the kind of case report cosmetic vendors don't mention.

GHRP-2 is a 6-amino-acid synthetic peptide developed in the 1990s as a growth hormone secretagogue. It mimics the endogenous hormone ghrelin at the GHSR receptor in the pituitary and hypothalamus, triggering pulsatile GH release. Short half-life, rapid onset, rapid clearance, designed for the kind of dosing pattern that aligns with natural GH pulsatility.

prose.header: the side effect profile that defines the tier
prose.body: GHRP-2 at therapeutic doses reliably elevates cortisol and prolactin alongside the GH pulse. Cortisol elevation is meaningful in bodybuilding and performance contexts where cortisol management is already a concern, chronic cortisol elevation works against anabolic goals and against subjective quality of life (sleep, mood, visceral fat). Prolactin elevation has its own implications: sexual dysfunction, gynecomastia risk, especially in users with underlying sensitivity. Appetite stimulation is part of ghrelin-receptor pharmacology and is more pronounced with GHRP-2 than with ipamorelin at equivalent GH-release magnitudes.

GHRP-2 users typically notice the appetite effect first, pronounced hunger soon after use. GH-release effects are discussed around sleep quality, recovery, skin, and body-composition changes, but the cortisol-related effects are part of the same receptor story. The overall pattern: the compound works, but users who've tried both typically report preferring ipamorelin.

prose.header: when GHRP-2 still has a niche
prose.body: Community discussion describes a narrow use case for GHRP-2: primarily users specifically seeking appetite stimulation during high-calorie phases, or users wanting the slightly more robust GH pulse compared to ipamorelin (the flipside of the receptor-binding profile difference). Across forum reports and peptide-clinic practice patterns, most discussion converges on ipamorelin having a cleaner side-effect profile for general use. None of this constitutes a recommendation for personal use. GHRP-2 is not FDA-approved for any human indication.

F-tier is for compounds that don't work or carry a risk profile wildly out of proportion to the use case. GHRP-2 works. The issue is that the chronic community-use risk record is not clean enough to hand-wave, and ipamorelin gives the same receptor lane with less endocrine noise. This is obsolete tier, not broken tier.

The most convincing modern use for GHRP-2 is as a diagnostic tool in endocrinology, not as a chronic body-composition peptide. Robust GH responses to GHRP-2 in adolescence make it a reliable provocation test for pituitary function. Clinical studies specifically evaluating GHRP-2 tests in hypothalamic-pituitary disorders support its use in diagnostic workup for GH deficiency. IGF-1 monitoring literature ties the compound's effects into how the downstream GH axis is actually tracked in clinical practice and in sports anti-doping contexts. The chronic community use case, repeated injections over weeks or months for physique enhancement, inherits the cortisol and prolactin side effects that the diagnostic single-dose context doesn't have to worry about. The pharmacology that makes GHRP-2 useful for endocrine testing is the same pharmacology that makes it suboptimal as a chronic physique peptide compared to ipamorelin.

GHRP-2 has picked up an unexpected second life in preclinical repair biology. a 2024 rotator-cuff study linked it to better tendon-bone healing and lower pro-inflammatory macrophage signaling, which widens the molecule beyond pure GH release. that's interesting science, but it still sits far from a real therapeutic program.

GHRP-6 is a 6-amino-acid peptide developed in the 1980s as one of the first synthetic ghrelin mimetics. Binds GHSR in the pituitary and hypothalamus, triggers pulsatile GH release, and, because it's hitting the same receptor that endogenous ghrelin hits to signal hunger, produces substantial appetite stimulation as a pharmacological feature rather than a side effect.

The appetite effect is the most distinctive thing about GHRP-6. Users report pronounced hunger within 20-40 minutes of injection, significantly stronger than GHRP-2 and unlike anything ipamorelin produces. In the 2000s this was marketed as a feature for bodybuilders trying to eat in surplus during bulking phases, when the problem isn't getting strong appetite. It's maintaining calorie-intake intensity over weeks of training. GHRP-6 solved that problem by overriding the natural satiety signal.

Users specifically wanting pronounced appetite stimulation during a high-calorie bulking phase are the narrow audience still talking about GHRP-6. The appetite-side-effect-as-feature framing applies. Whether that outweighs the cortisol and prolactin costs is a much harder question than the old forum shorthand makes it sound. For most use cases, ipamorelin made the answer simpler.

F-tier is for compounds that don't work or carry a risk profile wildly out of proportion to the use case. GHRP-6 works. The problem is that its appetite, cortisol, and prolactin package makes chronic community use a messy trade when a cleaner replacement exists. This is obsolete tier. The compound does what it claims; there is just a better option for essentially every use case.

prose.header: the tradeoff is intrinsic, not incidental
prose.body: The historical literature on GHRP-6 clarifies why it was displaced by ipamorelin in community use. Repeated IV administration studies document the full effect profile directly: GH release, ACTH elevation, cortisol elevation, and stage-2 sleep changes all occur together. Classical human studies from the late 1990s established that sleep, GH secretion, ACTH, and cortisol all move in response to the compound. A cardiovascular-system review places the pharmacology in a broader physiological frame. The takeaway across all of this: the appetite stimulation and stress-hormone elevation are not side effects. They're intrinsic features of ghrelin-receptor agonism at the potency GHRP-6 produces. That's the dividing line between GHRP-6 and the selective secretagogues that replaced it. Ipamorelin avoids the cortisol and prolactin cost by binding the receptor differently. No amount of dosing refinement fixes the GHRP-6 profile.

a 2024 intranasal study matters because it shows GHRP-6 can engage central ghrelin signaling after nasal delivery in mice. that's real pharmacology, not just forum folklore. it still doesn't give the compound a clean human efficacy package, but it does sharpen how the peptide may behave outside simple GH-release narratives.

AOD-9604 is an engineered version of the C-terminal region of human growth hormone (residues 176-191) with an N-terminal tyrosine added for improved manufacturability and stability. The design goal was to isolate GH's lipolytic activity, the part of the molecule that mobilizes fat from adipose tissue, while removing the anabolic effects that carry insulin resistance, joint pain, and IGF-1 elevation. On paper: targeted fat-loss peptide without GH's metabolic side effects. In practice: exactly what was designed, at a magnitude too small to matter clinically.

Metabolic Pharmaceuticals, an Australian biotech, developed AOD-9604 through a full pharmaceutical clinical program. Phase 1 safety studies passed, the selectivity claim was confirmed, no IGF-1 elevation, no hyperglycemia, clean tolerability. Phase 2a showed detectable lipolytic activity on metabolic markers. Phase 2b was the efficacy trial, the one that decides whether a compound graduates to phase 3. Phase 2b showed modest weight loss that was statistically detectable but clinically insignificant. The program was discontinued for the obesity indication around 2007. The failure wasn't about safety; it was about magnitude.

Community users of AOD-9604 generally report modest subjective fat-loss effects during cutting phases, which is consistent with the clinical trial story: a small signal that gets hard to distinguish from diet and training. The published human record used oral dosing, while the research-peptide market usually talks about injections. That route mismatch matters.

F-tier is for compounds that don't work at all, are actively harmful, or are fraudulent.
