AOD-9604 is none of those, it does exactly what it was designed to do, passed safety trials cleanly, and has legitimate regulatory recognition at the supplement level. The tier is about the magnitude problem: it works, but not enough to be worth using, especially when GLP-1s exist. 'It's real but not enough' is D-tier. 'It's fake or dangerous' is F-tier. The distinction matters.

AOD9604 is mostly a story about a plausible idea that failed to become a weight-loss drug. After the Metabolic Pharmaceuticals Phase 2b obesity trials underperformed, the molecule wandered into adjacent research niches. A development/update review lays out where the program stood once the original hype cooled. Later work explored intra-articular AOD9604 with or without hyaluronic acid in osteoarthritis, showing the molecule drifted into orthopedic experimentation after its obesity disappointment, not a validation of the weight-loss claim, but an example of how failed pharmaceuticals don't always fully disappear. Earlier preclinical lipolysis work in obese mice remains useful for contrasting the strong animal-model promise with the eventual modest human results. The modern literature doesn't rehabilitate AOD9604 as a fat-loss tool. It does explain why the molecule is still in circulation despite the clinical failure that should have ended the program.

AOD9604's newer footprint is narrower and stranger than the original fat-loss pitch. a rabbit OA study found intra-articular AOD9604 improved cartilage repair and worked even better when combined with hyaluronic acid, which suggests the fragment may have regenerative uses the market never centered. what it still doesn't have is a convincing human obesity comeback.

IGF-1 LR3 is engineered recombinant IGF-1 with two modifications: a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension (the 'Long' part) and an arginine-to-glutamate substitution at position 3 (the 'R3' part). The combined effect is reduced affinity for IGFBP (IGF binding proteins) and a dramatically extended plasma half-life, 20-30 hours versus roughly 12 minutes for native IGF-1. Originally developed as a cell culture reagent to maintain IGF-1 activity in serum-containing media.

The body keeps IGF-1 tightly regulated for reasons. Native IGF-1 is produced in response to GH signaling, bound to IGFBP-3 and ALS in circulation, released in controlled amounts to tissues, and cleared rapidly. This pulsatile regulation means IGF-1 signaling is robust enough for growth and repair but doesn't persist at elevated levels chronically. LR3 is specifically engineered to defeat this, reduced binding protein affinity means more free IGF-1, extended half-life means sustained elevation rather than pulses. That's the pharmacological appeal for muscle growth. It's also exactly the opposite of how the body's regulatory system is designed to work.

Community users report real muscle growth effects, the compound works at its claimed anabolic use case. Short-term adverse effects most commonly reported: hypoglycemia symptoms, injection-site lump formation, subjective reports of skin and feature changes after extended cycles, and occasional gastrointestinal changes consistent with bowel thickening. The pattern is predictable: the more chronic and supraphysiological the exposure story gets, the more the long-latency cancer-risk question matters. No user group is tracked systematically enough to answer that question.

prose.header: what legitimate IGF-1 elevation looks like
prose.body: Legitimate medical IGF-1 elevation usually enters through upstream endocrine pathways, GH or GH secretagogues that trigger native pulsatile IGF-1 production under a monitoring framework. The IGF-1 elevation achieved this way is more regulated, pulsatile, and binding-protein-buffered than LR3 exposure. That is the distinction this entry cares about: 'elevated IGF-1' is not the same claim as 'supraphysiological sustained unregulated IGF-1.'

D-tier is 'this doesn't really work' or 'this is obsoleted.' F-tier is 'the mechanism itself carries serious risk that the benefit doesn't justify.' IGF-1 LR3 is qualitatively different from the D-tier compounds. Dihexa's risk is speculative and pathway-based. GHRP-6 is obsoleted but not dangerous. AOD-9604 is weak but safe. LR3's cancer-risk mechanism is well-characterized in clinical epidemiology, the compound is specifically engineered to defeat the body's regulatory system, and the use case (bigger muscles faster) is not proportionate to the risk. F-tier. Stay away.

prose.header: monitoring and cancer signaling are the missing rigor
prose.body: The modern add-on literature on IGF-1 LR3 does not make the bodybuilding story look better. A 2024 preclinical study in late-gestation growth-restricted lambs found IGF-1 LR3 did not produce the growth benefit that intuition would predict, a negative translational result worth knowing about before extrapolating to healthy-adult physique enhancement. IGF-1 monitoring literature details the serology and assay work that legitimate IGF-1 elevation (via GH secretagogues, for example) is typically paired with, monitoring community users rarely perform. And contemporary cancer-biology work on the IGF-1/IGF-1R axis in triple-negative breast cancer extends the cancer-signaling concern past prostate and epithelial cancers into populations where the concern hits differently. The tier placement isn't about anecdotal aggression. It's about the combination of unmonitored supraphysiological IGF-1 elevation, a mechanism with robust cancer-risk signal, and a user base almost universally declining the monitoring infrastructure that would catch problems early.

the newer IGF-1 LR3 literature is a good antidote to easy anabolic storytelling. in a 2024 fetal-growth-restriction model, one week of treatment did not improve growth despite shifting amino-acid handling. that's useful because it shows how often IGF signaling changes biomarkers faster than it delivers the outcome people assume.

Follistatin is a regulatory protein that binds and inactivates members of the TGF-β superfamily, most famously myostatin (the primary negative regulator of muscle mass), but also activin A, activin B, GDF-11, and others. Two main isoforms exist: FS-315 (secreted, circulating) and FS-344 (membrane-associated, tissue-bound). Endogenous follistatin keeps myostatin signaling calibrated; animals with follistatin-overexpression or myostatin-knockout develop dramatic muscle hypertrophy. The Belgian Blue cattle phenotype, extreme muscularity, is a natural myostatin loss-of-function example, frequently cited in follistatin marketing.

Follistatin marketing positions it as a myostatin antagonist. The biology is broader. Follistatin binds activin A (regulator of cell proliferation, follicle development, immune function), activin B, GDF-11 (involved in aging and tissue homeostasis), and other TGF-β family proteins. Inactivating myostatin alone would be pharmacologically interesting and relatively constrained. Inactivating the broader TGF-β family regulators across an entire body over extended periods carries tissue-proliferation implications across multiple organ systems. This isn't a theoretical concern, TGF-β family signaling is central to cell-cycle regulation in epithelia, liver, reproductive tissues, and more.

Community reports on follistatin-344 are notably inconsistent. Some users report dramatic muscle gains after short exposure windows; others report nothing distinguishable from baseline; others report lumps at injection sites or local reactions. This inconsistency pattern is exactly what you'd expect from a product line where a significant fraction of vials don't actually contain the advertised compound. The users reporting dramatic effects might have received functional product; the users reporting nothing might have received nothing. Neither population can confirm which.

Myostatin inhibition as a therapeutic target is a legitimate area of pharmaceutical research, but the programs are small-molecule or monoclonal antibody based (bimagrumab, stamulumab, domagrozumab), not follistatin protein injection. Companies pursuing this space target myostatin specifically to avoid the off-target TGF-β concerns follistatin has. Clinical results have been mixed; the programs have faced challenges progressing through trials. The short version: the target is real, legitimate development is ongoing, and nobody serious is pursuing follistatin-344 injection as the approach.

F-tier is for compounds where the combination of risks, unknowns, and market-quality issues makes the use case indefensible. Follistatin-344 fits every condition: multi-target pharmacology with proliferation concerns, zero human safety data at use doses, widespread product misrepresentation, and a benefit claim that extrapolates from animal gene therapy to injected protein in ways the underlying biology doesn't support. Users are either taking a compound with unstudied tissue-proliferation effects or taking a vial that doesn't contain what it claims. Neither is a reasonable position.

prose.header: real science, wrong form for consumers
prose.body: The follistatin target space is legitimate. Phase 1/2a follistatin gene therapy in Becker muscular dystrophy demonstrated real clinical benefit in a serious medical-development setting, and follow-up gene therapy work showed improved ambulation in Becker MD patients. Both support that follistatin biology can produce meaningful outcomes when deployed through the right delivery mechanism. Engineered derivatives like the follistatin-derived myostatin inhibitory peptide DF-3 represent where serious researchers are heading, trying to tame the broad TGF-β family binding that makes wild-type follistatin pharmacologically messy. This is the right framing for the entry. The science is real and advancing. What's being sold as 'follistatin-344' injectable vials from research peptide vendors isn't the form the field trusts. Gene therapy works; engineered derivatives are in development; a 344-amino-acid protein in a grey-market vial is neither, even if the underlying target is legitimate.

recent follistatin work keeps widening the biology beyond myostatin and muscle size. a 2024 study found blood-pressure and vascular-structure benefits in hypertensive models, which makes the molecule more interesting scientifically and more complicated clinically. every new pathway it touches is another reason unsupervised use is harder to justify, not easier.

5-Amino-1MQ is a small molecule, not a peptide. It's a methylated quinolinium derivative, sold typically as the iodide salt, with adequate oral bioavailability in rodents. The 'peptide' framing is community shorthand and vendor marketing, not chemistry. What it does pharmacologically is inhibit nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT), an enzyme that consumes nicotinamide and SAM during methylation reactions and is highly expressed in obese adipose tissue.

NNMT methylates nicotinamide to 1-methylnicotinamide, consuming SAM in the process. In obesity, NNMT is upregulated in white adipose tissue and the liver. The pathway-level consequence: nicotinamide that would otherwise enter the NAD+ salvage pathway gets methylated and excreted, depleting NAD+ pools. SAM is also consumed, lowering methyl-donor availability. Inhibiting NNMT reverses both: more nicotinamide for NAD+ synthesis, more SAM for other methylation reactions, and a metabolic shift in adipocytes toward thermogenic phenotype. The Neelakantan 2018 paper showed this directly in HFD-obese mice over 11 days.

prose.header: why the compound has gathered attention despite the evidence gap
prose.body: interest in the compound is driven by a combination of factors that the published research describes: the NNMT pathway biology is well-characterized, the mouse-model efficacy is reproducible across multiple research groups, and the small-molecule oral framing offers an alternative to injectable peptides. None of these factors substitute for human clinical evidence, which is absent from the published literature.

user reports do exist. the character of the evidence is the issue. discussion is recent - most of it from 2022 forward. the loudest positive reports cluster in commercial channels (peptide-vendor blogs, podcasts running affiliate codes). the independent forum signal is mixed: about half the reports describe clear effects, about half describe nothing. nothing in any of it is the kind of data that would actually validate human efficacy: controlled comparison against placebo, DEXA or hydrostatic body composition, serial NAD+ measurement. that's not what community discussion produces, and it's not what's available here.

F-tier reflects compounds where published evidence indicates the marketing is fundamentally misleading or where documented safety risks materially outweigh the research basis. 5-Amino-1MQ does not fit either. The chemistry and preclinical research are legitimate and from established academic groups. The compound is at an earlier stage in the research-to-clinic pipeline than the marketing implies. If clinical data emerges and effects translate, the tier reflects that. The current tier reflects the current evidence: documented preclinical research, absent human clinical evidence.

the path to C-tier or higher is the path the published literature requires: registered phase-1 trial in humans, characterization of pharmacokinetics and short-term safety, then phase-2 efficacy work demonstrating that the mouse-observed metabolic effects translate. None of that infrastructure exists for 5-Amino-1MQ specifically as of 2026, although NNMT-inhibitor programs at the broader research level are continuing to advance. If a clinical-stage NNMT inhibitor enters trials in the next 24 months, the editorial assessment of the category will reflect that.

alerts.body: In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, BPC-157 was removed from category 2 because the nominations were withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC on July 23 2026 regarding BPC-157 acetate and free-base forms. That does not authorize compounding. Until PCAC acts and FDA finalizes, BPC-157 remains an unapproved new drug for U.S. compounding purposes. WADA's S0 ban remains unchanged, and the gray market continuing online is separate from legal pharmacy compounding.

FDA-approved (Ozempic 2017, Wegovy 2021, Rybelsus 2019; Wegovy HD 7.2 mg high-dose label added March 2026; high-dose oral semaglutide for obesity under FDA review post-OASIS-1). Off the FDA shortage list as of February 2025. Compounded semaglutide is no longer legal for most 503A pharmacies. 503B outsourcing facilities face ongoing litigation with Novo Nordisk. Medicare GLP-1 Bridge begins July 1, 2026 for eligible beneficiaries at $50/month. BALANCE is separate, with Medicaid as early as May 2026 and Medicare Part D in January 2027. NovoCare direct-to-patient $499/month launched March 2025.

FDA-approved: Mounjaro May 2022 (T2D), Zepbound Nov 2023 (obesity), Zepbound 2024 (OSA). KwikPen approved Feb 2026. Off the FDA shortage list since late 2024, so compounded tirzepatide is largely prohibited. CVS Caremark formulary exclusion from July 2025 is ongoing.

LillyDirect self-pay starts at $299 for 2.5 mg and rises by dose. Medicare GLP-1 Bridge starts July 1, 2026 at $50/month for eligible beneficiaries. BALANCE is a separate CMS model.

Not FDA-approved. TRIUMPH-4 readout December 11, 2025. TRANSCEND-T2D-1 positive March 2026. Additional phase III readouts are expected throughout 2026. Lilly has not announced an official NDA filing date. Any FDA timing depends on the full efficacy and safety package. No legal pharmacy access in the US. For sport, retatrutide should be treated as prohibited under WADA S0 while it remains an unapproved investigational drug.

FDA-approved (Victoza 2010 for T2D, Saxenda 2014 for obesity, Saxenda adolescent indication 2020). Authorized-generic Victoza launched June 2024. FDA approved the first ANDA generic referencing Victoza in December 2024. Generic Saxenda launched August 2025. Still on ASHP shortage list as of early 2026 due to Novo manufacturing. Medicare Part D does not cover Saxenda for weight loss. NovoCare savings card program reduces brand cost to $25-30/month for eligible commercially insured patients.

FDA-approved (Egrifta 2010, Egrifta SV 2014, Egrifta WR March 2025). Only approved indication: HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Label explicitly states: not for weight loss management, long-term cardiovascular safety not known. Orphan drug, no generic. Peptide clinic and gray-market off-label usage widespread but not FDA-sanctioned. 503A compounding restricted. WADA status: prohibited at all times.

FDA-approved across 11+ brands and 9 indications, pediatric and adult. WADA prohibited at all times for all sports. 21 U.S.C. § 333(e) makes non-approved distribution and possession-with-intent-to-distribute a five-year federal felony. personal use with a valid prescription is legal. 503A compounding pharmacies cannot compound HGH. over-the-counter "HGH" sprays, pills, and homeopathic products are not approved and in most cases contain no somatropin.

FDA-approved as Pregnyl, Novarel, Ovidrel, and generic chorionic gonadotropin. three indications: female infertility (ovulation induction), male hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, prepubertal cryptorchidism. reclassified as a biologic March 2020. 503A compounding pharmacies cannot compound it. WADA prohibits it in men (class S2), permits it in women. supply is still uneven by product and channel. Pregnyl has been allocation-limited. Novarel and generic chorionic gonadotropin have listed availability. homeopathic HCG products are not FDA-approved. FDA has issued warning letters to manufacturers making weight-loss claims.

In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, injectable GHK-Cu was removed from category 2 because the nominations were withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC before the end of February 2027 regarding potential inclusion on the 503A bulks list. Non-injectable/topical GHK-Cu follows a separate review track on the same timeline. That does not authorize injectable compounding; until PCAC acts and FDA finalizes, injectable GHK-Cu remains an unapproved new drug for U.S. compounding purposes. Topical cosmetic products containing GHK-Cu remain legally available under cosmetic regulation. WADA: not prohibited.

In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, TB-500 (thymosin beta-4 fragment) was removed from category 2 because the nomination was withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC on July 23 2026 regarding TB-500 acetate and TB-500 (free base). That does not authorize compounding; until PCAC acts and FDA finalizes, TB-500 remains an unapproved new drug for U.S. compounding purposes. WADA's S2 ban remains unchanged, and equine/racing detection programs are still active.

Not FDA-approved. Classified as an Investigational New Drug. WADA prohibited at all times (class S2). On October 29, 2024 the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) voted against 503A inclusion for ipamorelin and CJC-1295 followed the same pathway, citing impurity, immunogenicity, limited route-specific safety data, and serious adverse events including increased heart rate and systemic vasodilatory reactions. Available as research-grade peptide from laboratory suppliers for research use only. No legal pathway for human therapeutic use in the United States.

Not FDA-approved. Never completed a phase 3 program for any indication. WADA prohibited at all times (class S2). On October 29, 2024 the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) voted against 503A inclusion for ipamorelin, citing immunogenicity, peptide impurity, limited route-specific safety data, and serious adverse events including a death in the context of an intravenous gastric-motility study. Available as research-grade peptide from laboratory suppliers for research use only. No legal pathway for human therapeutic use in the United States.

former FDA approval (Geref 1990 diagnostic, 1997 pediatric GHD therapeutic). EMD Serono pulled it in 2008. FDA 2013 ruling: NOT withdrawn for safety or effectiveness. no current FDA-approved commercial product. compounding pharmacies can legally produce sermorelin on stronger footing than never-approved peptides, though compounded formulations are not FDA-reviewed. no approved adult indication. WADA prohibited at all times (class S2).

FDA-approved as Vyleesi (bremelanotide) June 21 2019 for acquired generalized HSDD in premenopausal women. Prescription required. Off-label physician-supervised use for men is legal but not FDA-sanctioned. 503A compounding pharmacies restricted from commercial compounding since 2023 FDA guidance. Research-grade PT-141 available from laboratory suppliers for research use only. WADA: not specifically listed as prohibited in 2026 code. Contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension or known cardiovascular disease; pregnancy is handled as a warning and discontinuation issue, not the same label bucket.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. FDA has issued warning letters to vendors marketing it. UK MHRA, Australian TGA, and multiple European regulatory agencies have issued consumer warnings. 503A compounding pharmacies cannot legally compound it. Available only as research-grade peptide for research use. Gray-market sales through social media, gyms, and aesthetic clinics persist despite regulatory action. Related compound afamelanotide (Melanotan I) IS FDA-approved as Scenesse for erythropoietic protoporphyria. This is a different molecule.

Not FDA-approved as a combination product. None of the four component peptides (KPV, GHK-Cu, BPC-157, TB-500) is FDA-approved for therapeutic human use. On April 22, 2026 the FDA formally removed BPC-157, TB-500, KPV, and injectable GHK-Cu from 503A category 2. PCAC review is scheduled for July 23, 2026 (first three) and before February 2027 (GHK-Cu); public comment closes July 9. Available as research-grade peptide blend from laboratory suppliers for research use. No legal pathway for human therapeutic use in the United States. WADA prohibited by extension of component bans (class S2).

Not FDA-approved as a combination product. None of the three components is FDA-approved for therapeutic human use. On April 22, 2026 the FDA formally removed BPC-157, TB-500, and injectable GHK-Cu from 503A category 2. PCAC review is scheduled for July 23, 2026 (BPC-157, TB-500) and before February 2027 (injectable GHK-Cu); public comment closes July 9. Available as research-grade peptide blend from laboratory suppliers for research use only. No legal pathway for human therapeutic use in the United States. For sport, BPC-157 and TB-500 make the blend prohibited even though GHK-Cu is not specifically prohibited.

Analytically, blends are harder to verify than single peptides. The 4.7-to-1 mass ratio between CJC-1295 (3367.9 Da) and ipamorelin (711.85 Da) means the two molecules behave differently on HPLC columns and ionize differently in mass spectrometry. Well-sourced clinical blends from 503A-compliant compounding pharmacies handle this with validated methods and certificates of analysis. Research-peptide market vials vary widely, some are excellent, some are mislabeled, and the Janvier 2018 Belgian market study confirmed that both CJC-1295 and ipamorelin appear in falsified or under-potency product. In blend discussions, the source is the variable that matters.

Neither CJC-1295 nor ipamorelin is FDA-approved. Both are classified as research-use-only. On October 29, 2024 the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) voted against 503A inclusion for ipamorelin, citing immunogenicity, peptide impurity, limited route-specific safety data, and serious adverse events including a death in an intravenous gastric-motility study. CJC-1295 followed the same pathway. Both compounds are on the WADA Prohibited List (Section S2), banned in competition for tested athletes. Possession for personal research is legal in most U.S. jurisdictions; marketing for human therapeutic use is not.

Neither BPC-157 nor TB-500 is FDA-approved for any healing indication. BPC-157 is prohibited in sport under WADA S0 as an unapproved substance, while TB-500 / thymosin-beta-4-related products sit in the peptide hormone and growth-factor orbit. FDA's April 22 2026 503A update removed BPC-157 from category 2 after the nomination was withdrawn and scheduled PCAC review for BPC-157 forms. That does not authorize routine compounding. Pre-mixed 'Wolverine Blend' vials are widely available through research-peptide suppliers but cannot legally be sold for human use.

Registered in Russia for ischemic stroke and selected neurocognitive or ophthalmic indications. Listed on the Russian Vital & Essential Drugs List since 2011. Not FDA-approved for any indication in the United States. In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, Semax was removed from category 2 because the nominations were withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC on July 24 2026 regarding Semax acetate and Semax (free base). That review matters for U.S. compounding status, but it is not approval. Semax is not specifically listed on the WADA 2026 Prohibited List.

Registered in Russia since 2009 for generalized anxiety disorder and neurasthenia. Available through Russian pharmacy channels as an intranasal spray. Not FDA-approved for any indication in the United States. Classified as a research chemical in the U.S. No currently scheduled FDA review. Not on the WADA Prohibited List. Unlike Semax, Selank is not currently under FDA 503A compounding review, though the Russian clinical evidence base is structurally similar.

GI adverse events occurred in 79.6% of cagrisema patients in REDEFINE 1 vs 39.9% on placebo, higher than semaglutide monotherapy rates in previous trials. Nausea (55%), constipation (30.7%), vomiting (26.1%). Mostly mild-to-moderate and transient, but clinically noticeable. Discontinuation rates due to adverse events remained low: 5.9% cagrisema vs 3.5% placebo in REDEFINE 1, 8.4% vs 3% in REDEFINE 2. Adding the amylin analog does increase GI burden on top of semaglutide. Patients already struggling with semaglutide tolerability may find cagrisema harder.

NDA filed with the FDA on December 18, 2025. Review currently underway. Standard FDA review timelines point toward a possible late-2026 decision, but no approval has issued as of May 2026. REDEFINE-4 complicated the commercial story by missing non-inferiority versus tirzepatide after the NDA filing. The drug is not currently available outside clinical trial enrollment in the REDEFINE program. Until approval, cagrisema exists only as an investigational product.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Not EMA-approved. Not currently in any late-stage regulatory pipeline. Clinical research is ongoing primarily at Imperial College London and a handful of other academic centers. Kisspeptin-54 is sometimes used in UK research and clinical settings under investigator protocols. Kisspeptin-10 in the U.S. is classified as a research chemical. WADA's 2026 Prohibited List covers kisspeptin and its agonist analogues under testosterone-stimulating peptides in males.

FDA-approved as Pitocin for labor induction, labor augmentation, and postpartum hemorrhage control, 1962. No other FDA-approved indications. Intranasal oxytocin products are sold in various international markets under different brand names. U.S. intranasal oxytocin use is via compounding pharmacy prescription or research-peptide sources. Not on the WADA Prohibited List. Compounding status secure given long-established pharmaceutical use. Multiple phase 2 trials ongoing for non-obstetric indications as of 2026.

In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, MOTS-c was removed from category 2 because the nomination was withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC on July 23 2026 regarding MOTS-c acetate and MOTS-c (free base). That is the first formal FDA review track scheduled for MOTS-c, but it does not authorize compounding. MOTS-c itself remains an unapproved new drug for U.S. compounding purposes. For tested athletes, the unapproved-drug status creates WADA S0 risk. Human data still comes closest through CB4211, a MOTS-c analog that completed Phase 1a/1b in 2021.

FDA approved Forzinity (elamipretide) for Barth syndrome on September 19, 2025, specifically to improve muscle strength in adult and pediatric patients weighing at least 30 kg. Accelerated approval pathway. First FDA approval for a mitochondria-targeted peptide in history. Multiple prior regulatory designations: Fast Track for primary mitochondrial myopathy (2016), Orphan Drug designation for Barth syndrome (2017), Fast Track for Leber's Hereditary Optic Neuropathy (2017). Expanded access exists for some confirmed genetic mitochondrial disease patients outside the Barth approval. Research-peptide SS-31 is available for laboratory research. Clinical use outside Barth indication is off-label.

In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, KPV was removed from category 2 because the nomination was withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC on July 23 2026 regarding KPV acetate and KPV (free base). That does not authorize compounding. Until PCAC acts and FDA finalizes, KPV remains an unapproved new drug for U.S. compounding purposes. It is not on the WADA Prohibited List.

NAD+ and its precursors are not FDA-approved drugs for anti-aging, fatigue, cognition, or longevity. NMN's U.S. supplement status changed after the 2022 FDA letter; FDA reinstated NMN's dietary-supplement NDI status in December 2025, so NMN is again marketed as a supplement. NAD+ and NADH appear in FDA's April 22 2026 503A category 1 document, which means interim enforcement discretion while the substances are evaluated, not approval. IV NAD+ is administered through longevity and integrative clinics. Not on the WADA Prohibited List.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Classified as a research chemical in the United States. In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, Epitalon was removed from category 2 because the nominations were withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC on July 24 2026 regarding Epitalon acetate and Epitalon (free base). That does not authorize compounding; until PCAC acts and FDA finalizes, it remains an unapproved new drug for U.S. compounding purposes. Not on the WADA Prohibited List. Russian bioregulator use still does not translate to Western approval.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Classified as a research chemical in the United States. No formal regulatory approval in Russia equivalent to semax or selank. Not on the WADA Prohibited List. Limited commercial presence compared to other Khavinson-program peptides, availability through research-peptide vendors is inconsistent.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Not a controlled substance. Classified as a research chemical in the United States. In FDA's updated April 22 2026 503A categories document, Emideltide (DSIP) was removed from category 2 because the nominations were withdrawn, and FDA says it intends to consult the PCAC on July 24 2026 regarding Emideltide acetate and Emideltide (free base). That does not authorize compounding; until PCAC acts and FDA finalizes, it remains an unapproved new drug for U.S. compounding purposes. Not on the WADA Prohibited List.

Not FDA-approved for any indication in the United States. Approved as Zadaxin in approximately 35 countries, primarily for chronic hepatitis B adjunct therapy. Multiple FDA approval attempts have not succeeded. FDA's December 2024 PCAC reviewed thymosin alpha-1-related bulk substances and voted against 503A bulks-list inclusion, leaving U.S. compounding access constrained and legally unsettled. U.S. availability is mostly research-peptide or narrowly supervised off-label clinic channels, not a clean routine-compounding lane. Not on the WADA Prohibited List.

Gonadorelin has a real FDA pedigree through Factrel diagnostic testing and historical Lutrepulse pulsatile ovulation-induction use, but those branded finished products are not active mainstream U.S. commercial products today. FDA's April 22, 2026 503A categories document does not place gonadorelin acetate in category 1, so historical approval should not be confused with current bulks-list status. Current U.S. access is mainly compounded prescription use or research-vendor supply, which should be separated from FDA-approved finished-product availability. Not a controlled substance. WADA's 2026 Prohibited List covers gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH, gonadorelin) and agonist analogues as testosterone-stimulating peptides in males, prohibited at all times.

FDA-approved as Trelstar (advanced prostate cancer) and Triptodur (central precocious puberty). Approved in Europe as Decapeptyl for broader gynecological indications. Prescription-only medication in all regulated markets. Grey-market availability exists through research-peptide vendors in the US. WADA's 2026 Prohibited List covers GnRH agonist analogues, including triptorelin, as testosterone-stimulating peptides in males, prohibited at all times.

FDA-approved as Scenesse (afamelanotide) in 2019 for erythropoietic protoporphyria, administered as a controlled-release subcutaneous implant by qualified dermatologists. EU approval since 2014 for the same indication. Also approved in Australia (TGA) and Switzerland. Not approved for cosmetic tanning or any other indication in any regulated market. Reconstituted melanotan I from research-peptide vendors is a research chemical in the US. Dermatology societies in the UK, Australia, and elsewhere have issued warnings specifically about cosmetic melanotan use and increased melanocytic lesion monitoring needs.

Not a regulated pharmaceutical for any indication. Classified as a cosmetic ingredient in most jurisdictions. Present in numerous topical hair products globally. Not FDA-approved as a hair-loss treatment. Not on the WADA Prohibited List. No specific safety concerns in topical cosmetic use at typical formulation concentrations, though injection-grade use has not been formally characterized.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Research chemical classification in the US. Available through research-peptide vendors, typically marketed alongside (or as a cheaper alternative to) full TB-500. WADA's 2026 list covers thymosin beta-4 and related derivatives such as TB-500, so tested athletes should treat the LKKTETQ fragment as sport-prohibited unless an anti-doping authority says otherwise.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. AOD9604 (the modified fragment cousin) has a GRAS safety determination for intended ingredient use in some contexts but is not approved as a pharmaceutical and has no cleared weight-loss claim. The unmodified 176-191 fragment is classified as a research chemical. WADA's 2026 Prohibited List covers hGH fragments, including hGH 176-191 and AOD-9604, under S2. Available through research-peptide vendors.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Not progressed to human trials by the original developer. Classified as a research chemical. Related compound fosgonimeton went into phase 2/3 Alzheimer's trials with Athira Pharma, but late-stage readouts did not meet primary endpoints and Athira moved away from the program after restructuring and rebranding to LeonaBio. FDA has also placed Dihexa acetate into future compounding-status review, which is a pharmacy-compounding question, not clinical validation. Grey-market availability persists through nootropic and research-peptide vendors. Not a controlled substance.

Not FDA-approved as a drug for any indication. Regulated as a cosmetic ingredient, legal in skincare formulations without prescription. Used at 2-5% concentration in commercial products. Not on any controlled-substance or prohibited-substance list. Standard cosmetic-industry safety documentation.

Oral glutathione: supplement status in the US. Injectable glutathione for skin lightening: not FDA-approved in the US, and Philippines DOH/FDA states that injectable glutathione is not approved there for skin lightening even though it has an approved chemotherapy-adjunct context. Compounded sterile injectables raise a separate quality-control issue; FDA has warned about endotoxin-related adverse events from L-glutathione powder used in compounding. Not a controlled substance.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Investigated through Phase 2 clinical trials in the 1990s-2000s for GH-deficiency indications without achieving approval. Available through research-peptide vendors. Not a controlled substance. Falls under WADA Prohibited List S2 (peptide hormones, growth factors, related substances) for competitive athletes, prohibited both in and out of competition.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. Investigated in 1980s-1990s clinical programs without achieving approval. Available through research-peptide vendors. Not a controlled substance. Falls under WADA Prohibited List S2 (peptide hormones, growth factors, related substances), prohibited both in and out of competition for athletes.

Not FDA-approved as a drug for any indication. GRAS status is food-ingredient safety positioning, not efficacy certification, not drug approval, and not a 503A compounding green light. Available through supplement vendors, peptide clinics, and research-chemical suppliers. WADA's 2026 Prohibited List names AOD-9604 under hGH fragments in class S2, prohibited at all times for covered athletes.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. No legitimate human therapeutic use, LR3 was developed as a cell culture reagent. Classified as a research chemical. Available through peptide-vendor grey market. Falls under WADA Prohibited List S2 (peptide hormones, growth factors, related substances), prohibited both in and out of competition for athletes. In some jurisdictions, performance-enhancement use may trigger additional regulatory scrutiny beyond the baseline research-chemical framework.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. No legitimate pharmaceutical development for performance use. Classified as a research chemical. Available through peptide-vendor grey market with significant product quality concerns. Falls under WADA Prohibited List S2 (peptide hormones, growth factors, related substances), prohibited both in and out of competition. Myostatin-inhibitor pharmaceutical programs (bimagrumab, etc.) are separate regulatory matters and are not substitutes for grey-market follistatin products.

Not FDA-approved for any indication. No registered clinical-stage development program as of 2026. Sold as a research chemical via peptide and biotech-supply vendors. Not on any controlled-substance list. Not a drug, supplement, or food under FDA classification. Standard 'not for human consumption' research-chemical labeling applies.

callouts.body: tendon, ligament, GI mucosa, and vascular endothelium.

list $1,349/mo → NovoCare direct $499/mo (March 2025) → Wegovy HD via GoodRx $399/mo (April 2026) → Medicare BALANCE cap $50/mo copay (2026). compounded gray-market ran $200-400/mo until enforcement tightened.
users consistently report "food noise" quieting. this is real and distinct from just feeling less hungry. GLP-1 receptors in the brain appear to be doing most of the work.
many users report fat cravings specifically fall off a cliff. this appears to be a GIP-pathway effect, doesn't show up on sema the same way.
resting energy expenditure increases ~6%. sema and tirz do not do this. it's a direct glucagon-pathway effect.
visceral fat is the dangerous kind. it tracks with cardiovascular risk, insulin resistance, and early mortality far more strongly than subcutaneous fat or BMI alone.

this is why peptides like tesamorelin, sermorelin, and CJC-1295 exist, they stimulate the body's own GH release, preserving the natural pulse pattern. HGH itself is a direct replacement; the GHRH analogs are upstream.

testosterone replacement therapy suppresses the HPG axis. the pituitary stops making LH. the testes stop making their own testosterone and begin to shrink. HCG skips the pituitary and signals the testes directly, keeping them working through the cycle.
GHK-Cu shows unusually broad gene-expression signatures in cell and database analyses. that supports systems-biology interest, not proof that a serum or injectable vial rewrites the whole body.

does the 7-aa active site reproduce the full 43-aa protein's biology? some activity yes, actin binding is preserved. some probably not, because half-life, tissue retention, and protein-protein interactions all differ. this is why human evidence matters, and why its absence matters.

endogenous GH is released in pulses, roughly 10-15 per day, the largest during deep sleep. the body's downstream tissues evolved to respond to that pulse pattern. CJC-1295 DAC produces continuous GH elevation, which may desensitize downstream signaling over time. the no-DAC version preserves more of the natural pulse pattern. this is why peptide clinic culture has increasingly favored no-DAC CJC-1295 + ipamorelin over the original DAC version.

GHRH activates one receptor (GHRH-R) on pituitary somatotrophs. Ghrelin/ipamorelin activates a different receptor (GHS-R1a) on the same cells. Stimulating both simultaneously produces more GH than either alone. CJC-1295 provides sustained GHRH signaling, ipamorelin provides the acute ghrelin signal, the combination approximates a larger, longer natural pulse.

the short half-life is a feature, not a bug. the pituitary has built-in negative feedback via somatostatin. when GH rises too high, somatostatin shuts off further release. sermorelin is transient enough that the feedback still works. that is the pharmacology that sets it apart from exogenous HGH.

callouts.header: why this matters for the off-label use case
callouts.body: sildenafil and tadalafil don't address desire, they only enable erection when desire is present. PT-141 addresses the desire pathway upstream. for men whose ED has a psychological/desire component rather than purely vascular, PT-141 targets a different bottleneck. this is why peptide clinics and men's health practices adopted it despite the lack of FDA approval for male use.

MT2 was designed to prevent skin cancer by replacing the need for UV-induced tanning. in practice, MT2 users often combine it with tanning bed sessions to activate the peptide, which dramatically increases UV exposure. real-world use patterns actively oppose the original protective hypothesis.

KLOW is marketed as multi-pathway synergy. whether it's actually synergistic (1+1=3) or merely additive (1+1=2) hasn't been tested. peptide clinic clinical experience is the evidence base, which is neither placebo-controlled nor systematically tracked.

GLOW is specifically marketed for cosmetic and aesthetic use cases. post-laser resurfacing recovery. fine line reduction. skin quality. hair support. the underlying mechanisms are the same as KLOW minus gut/inflammation, the difference is cosmetic framing vs healing-stack framing for effectively the same chemistry.

ipamorelin provides the acute ghrelin-receptor signal. CJC-1295 supplies the GHRH-side signal. together they can amplify GH output through receptor-pair pharmacology. peptide clinics did not invent this mechanism; they noticed it and built practice patterns around it.
BPC-157: the local repair story. TB-500: the migration story. That is the cleanest way to understand why the blend caught on. It is a model of mechanism, not proof of outcome.

take the part of ACTH that makes rats smarter. cut out the part that makes them stressed. add a stability tail so it survives long enough to matter. the result is a peptide with the cognitive profile of a hormone but none of the endocrine side effects.

tuftsin was supposed to be an immune peptide. adding a stabilizer tail to extend its half-life was supposed to just make it last longer. what it actually did was reveal an anxiolytic effect that the parent molecule didn't have. the mechanism of that unexpected property is still being mapped.

cagrilintide alone gave 11.8% weight loss. semaglutide alone gave 16.1%. cagrisema produced 22.7%, which supports a real combination effect without proving that the two pathways are more than additive. the drug works. the word synergy should be earned carefully.

kisspeptin → GnRH → LH/FSH → testosterone/estrogen. kisspeptin is the starter motor. every other reproductive hormone downstream depends on kisspeptin firing first. turn off kisspeptin signaling, the whole reproductive axis goes offline. this is how puberty works in reverse, and it's why kisspeptin is therapeutically interesting across fertility, contraception, HSDD, and delayed puberty.

1953, cornell medical college, vincent du vigneaud. nine amino acids assembled chemically to produce a biologically active hormone. nobel prize two years later. the achievement is why peptides are a pharmaceutical category at all, before this, hormones were extracts. after this, they were drugs.

MOTS-c moves from mitochondria to nucleus and directly influences gene expression there. the mitochondria aren't just receiving instructions from the nucleus. They're sending their own. this is a genuine shift in how cellular regulation is understood.

SS-31 preferentially accumulates in mitochondria with compromised cardiolipin. it doesn't meaningfully affect healthy mitochondria. that selectivity, finding damaged mitochondria and fixing them without interfering with the healthy ones, is why the side effect profile is so clean.

α-MSH: anti-inflammatory + pigmentation + appetite suppression + arousal. KPV: just anti-inflammatory. same effect, fewer side effects, smaller molecule, orally absorbable. one of the few cases where trimming a peptide down makes it a better drug candidate than the full version.

oral NMN and NR, robust evidence that they raise blood NAD+ levels. modest evidence for metabolic benefit. IV NAD+, popular, expensive, less rigorous clinical trial data. direct NAD+ supplementation in all forms has to navigate cellular uptake problems. precursors are more efficient on a pharmacokinetic basis than direct NAD+.

the dominant commercial story is that epithalon activates telomerase and thereby extends lifespan. the primary evidence is in vitro work from the Khavinson lab showing telomerase upregulation in cultured cells. this is genuinely interesting data. it is not 'proof of human lifespan extension,' which is what vendors sell it as. those are two very different claims.

short peptides can cross the blood-brain barrier. This is established for some compounds. whether pinealon specifically does so at meaningful concentrations in humans at typical doses is a different question, and one the published evidence does not cleanly answer.

most peptides have a known receptor or a known signaling pathway. DSIP doesn't. no confirmed receptor has ever been identified. its biological effects are real enough to have kept research interest alive for decades, but the molecular mechanism remains poorly characterized. that absence is itself part of why the clinical development never consolidated.

callouts.header: the gap between approved and used
callouts.body: if someone in Italy gets thymosin alpha-1 at a hospital, they almost certainly have chronic hepatitis B and are getting it as part of an antiviral protocol. if someone in the US gets thymosin alpha-1 from a peptide vendor, they almost certainly do not have hepatitis B. the compound is being used for entirely different purposes than what it's approved to treat. the evidence base is for the first use case, not the second.

GnRH works because it's pulsatile. your hypothalamus releases it every 90-120 minutes in precisely timed bursts, and the pituitary responds to that rhythm. gonadorelin's 2-4 minute half-life means a single injection is more like a pulse than a baseline, but standard TRT-community dosing (every other day, subcutaneous) is neither pulsatile enough to mimic the physiological rhythm nor sustained enough to provide continuous stimulation. the pharmacology and the practical dosing are working against each other.

the PCT theory tries to harvest the early flare before the later suppression phase arrives. for someone coming off a steroid cycle with a suppressed HPG axis, that spike is supposed to jolt the testes back into endogenous testosterone production and kickstart recovery. the cited evidence is narrow. when the pharmacology goes the other way, partial desensitization without a clean restart is the failure mode.

callouts.header: the approval versus the street use
callouts.body: Scenesse is a controlled-release subcutaneous implant placed by a dermatologist, one every two months, for patients with a specific rare disease. street melanotan I is a reconstituted lyophilized powder self-injected from research-peptide vials by people who want to tan faster. same active molecule. different drug, functionally, in terms of dose, delivery, monitoring, and safety data.

'copper peptide' is a category that includes molecules with very different evidence bases. GHK-Cu has trials, PubMed heft, and cosmeceutical integration at major brands. AHK-Cu has a much narrower literature and is often sold by riding the copper-peptide category's overall reputation. these are not the same compound and the evidence is not interchangeable.

a car engine is what makes a car go. you can take the engine out and put it on a stand and run it. it will produce torque. it won't drive you anywhere. TB-500 frag is the engine; full TB-500 is the car. the engine works. whether the engine alone does the job people buy the car for is a different question.

the grey market mostly sells unmodified 176-191 (no N-terminal tyrosine). AOD9604 is the modified version that was actually clinically trialed. the unmodified fragment was the starting material, not the clinical endpoint. the community often treats them as interchangeable, but only AOD9604 has formal human trial data, and that data is what killed the obesity indication.

HGF/c-Met is not a side pathway. It's one of the primary receptor tyrosine kinase pathways that cancer cells co-opt for proliferation and metastasis. Multiple approved oncology drugs (crizotinib, cabozantinib, capmatinib) exist specifically to inhibit c-Met signaling in tumors. Dihexa activates it. Chronically. In healthy people. With no human pharmacokinetic data.

the SNAP-8 marketing category includes phrases like "botox without the needles" and "muscle-relaxing cream." the SNAP-8 pharmacology delivers, at best, a slight surface-texture improvement measurable in controlled studies as low-single-digit percentage changes in wrinkle metrics. those two framings are not describing the same product.

Skin brightening is real enough to discuss and weak enough to keep restrained. Oral and topical studies show modest, variable changes, mostly in sun-exposed areas. IV clinic protocols are a different thing: the Philippines DOH/FDA says injectable glutathione is not approved for skin lightening, lacks published dosing guidelines for that purpose, and carries safety concerns. In the US, FDA flagged adverse events from compounded injectable glutathione linked to endotoxin exposure. The route is the risk story.

callouts.header: ipamorelin did the same job cleaner
callouts.body: GHSR is the target. ipamorelin binds it and triggers GH release without the cortisol spike, the prolactin spike, or the appetite storm. same price. same availability. same injection frequency. the entire case for GHRP-2 collapses into "we had it first."

callouts.header: two compounds clean beats one compound messy
callouts.body: the GHRP-6 pitch is that it does two things: stimulates GH, stimulates appetite. the 2026 counter-pitch is cleaner: ipamorelin is the better GH signal, while GHRP-6 is mostly an appetite-stimulation relic. doing two jobs with one messy compound was the only option in 2005. it isn't in 2026.

callouts.header: GRAS status is not drug approval
callouts.body: AOD-9604 has a GRAS food-ingredient safety record, not FDA drug approval, not weight-loss efficacy clearance, and not a 503A compounding green light. supplement-market marketing that implies 'FDA-cleared weight-loss peptide' is misrepresenting the regulatory status.

IGF-1 elevation is not a theoretical cancer risk. It's one of the most consistently replicated associations in clinical epidemiology. Elevated circulating IGF-1 is associated with increased risk of prostate cancer, breast cancer, colorectal cancer, and lung cancer across multiple large cohort studies. the magnitude of association varies by cancer type. the direction does not. running sustained supraphysiological IGF-1 for muscle growth, systemically, for months, in healthy adults, is the clearest example in the peptide space of a benefit that doesn't justify the mechanism.

follistatin-344 is a 344-amino-acid protein with complex secondary and tertiary structure. reliable production requires recombinant expression systems, proper folding, and cold-chain stability, conditions most research-peptide suppliers don't actually maintain. independent testing of grey-market follistatin products has repeatedly found inconsistent follistatin content, degraded protein, or entirely different compounds. users have no way to know whether they're injecting functional follistatin-344, degraded protein, or something else entirely. this is distinct from the pharmacological concerns. It's a market-quality concern that by itself should make the compound uninteresting.

every peptide and small-molecule compound on this site has a 'mouse-to-human gap' section because the gap is real and the compounds that close it (semaglutide, tirzepatide, retatrutide) had to do thousands of patient-years of clinical work to do so. 5-Amino-1MQ is at step zero of that. zero registered human trials. zero published PK data in humans. zero efficacy data in humans. the compound is at the stage where the question is 'does NNMT inhibition do the same thing in human adipose tissue that it does in mouse adipose tissue,' and we don't have the answer.
